question: At which one of the following places are cave paintings of prehistoric times found?
explanation: The cave painting of prehistoric times was found at Bhimbetka in the Raisen district, Madhya Pradesh. These caves are named after 'Bhima', one of the five Pandavas of Mahabharta. Bhimbetka simply means "sitting place of Bhima". It was discovered by Dr. V. S. Wakankar in 1958. It is located inside the Ratapani Wild Life Sanctuary and was declared as 'World Heritage Site' in 2003. The entire region comprises more than 600 caves.

question: Which of the following period is significant for the beginning of agriculture?
explanation: Agriculture likely began during the Neolithic era/age before roughly 9000 BC. When polished stone tools were developed and the last ice age ended. Historians have several theories about why many societies switched from hunting and foraging to settled agriculture. Based on current archaeological evidence, atomically modern humans have existed roughly 200000–300000 years. However, before roughly 15000–20000 years ago, we have no evidence that our ancestors had agriculture.

question: The discovery of Augustino coins help to confirm further the trade contacts with Rome at
explanation: The discovery of Augustino coins help to confirm further the trade contacts with Rome at Arikamedu. Significant findings at Arikamedu indude numerous Indo-pacific beads, which facilitated fixing the period of its origin. Arikamedu is an Archaeological Site in Southern India, in Kakkayanthope Ariyankuppam Commune, Puducherry.

question: Palaeolithic stone tool was first discovered in India in 1863 by
explanation: Palaeolithic stone tool was first discovered in India in 1863 by Robert Bruce from Pallavaram. 22nd Subba Rao– He was the Governor of Reserve Bank of India.

question: Which one of the following is the correct chronological order regarding to Invaders in ancient India?
explanation: The correct chronological order regarding to Invaders in ancient India : Greeks-Sakas-Kushans. The Indo-Greeks were the first to issue Gold coins in India. The Greeks were followed by the Sakas. Sakas were divided into five branches. The Parthian rule was followed by Kushans rules two successive dynasty first one was ruled till 78 AD.

question: 'Soan culture' is another name for ................
explanation: Soan valley was first made by Dr. D. N. Wadia and thereafter by Dr. Helmut De Terrain 1928 and 1932 respectively. The Soanian is an archaeological culture of the lower Paleolithic in Sivalik hills of the Indian subcontinent. Soanian sites are found along the Sivalik region in present day India, Nepal and Pakistan. The Lower Paleolithic is the earliest subdivision of the old stone age.

question: In which year was the wheel invented?
explanation: It is believed that the first wheel was invented by the Mesopotamians in 3500 BC. However, it is also said that it was first developed by the Chinese civilization around 2800 BCE.

question: The biggest cave group of M.P is
explanation: The biggest cave group of M.P. is Bhimbetka caves. It is located in the Raisen district in the Indian state of Madhya Pradesh about 45 kilometers, South- East of Bhopal. It is a UNESCO world Heritage sites in India. Note:- It has the oldest known rock art in India as well as is one of the largest prehistoric complexes. 17 / 896

question: The oldest deciphered script of the Indian subcontinent is
explanation: The oldest deciphered script is Brahmi, dating to around 4th century BCE. In South India, the oldest deciphered script is Tamil-Brahmi which is dated to two centuries after Brahmi. • Harappan– Known as Indus script is a corpus of symbols produced by Indus Valley Civilization. • Kharoshti– It was an ancient Indian script used by the Khasa, Saka and Yuezhi peoples. • Devanagari– Based on Indian ancient Brahmi script. Modern Hindi.

question: In ancient times the term "Talapatra" referred to
explanation: Talapatra is referred to palm leaf manuscripts are manuscripts made out of dried palm leaves. Palm leaves were used as writing materials in Indian subcontinent and in south-east Asia reportedly dating 5th back to the century BCE.

question: The caves of Bhim Betka are famous for one of the following elements :
explanation: The Bhim Betka site has the oldest known rock art in India, as well as is one of the largest prehistoric complexes. Bhim Betka rock art is considered oldest petroglyphs in the world, some of these similar to aboriginal rock art in Australia and the Paleolithic Lascaux Cave paintings in France. The shelters were designated a UNESCO World Heritage site in 2003.

question: Which was the first metal used by man?
explanation: Copper was first used by man over 10,000 years ago. A copper pendant discovered in what is now Northern Iraq has been dated about 8700 B.C. for nearly five millennia copper was the only metal known to man, and thus had all the metal applications.

question: From which Indus Valley site has the evidence of a ploughed field been found?
explanation: Kalibangan (An Indus Valley Site) excavations in present Western Rajasthan shows a ploughed field, the first site of this nature in the world. It shows a Grid Pattern of famous placed about 30 cm an apart running east-west and other spaced about 190 cm apart running North-South, a pattern remarkable similar to the one practiced even now. Kalibangan is a town located on the left or southern banks of Ghaggar river between the Suratgarh and Hanumangarh in Hanumangarh district,Rajasthan.

question: Which was the port town of the Indus Valley Civilization?
explanation: The Indus Valley Civilisation was a Bronze Age Civilisation. It is flourished in the basin of the Indus river. The Indus people worshipped Pashupati Nath Shiva. The deity Shiva Pashupati had been found on clay seals unearthed. Pashupati Mahadeva was the chief male deity of people of Indus valley civilization. The Pashupati seal founded at Mohenjo-daro had an image of Pashupati Mahadeva with three face two horns sitting on a low throne. He was surrounded by a tiger, a rhino, a buffalo and an elephant with two deers near his feet. It was also called as Bronze Age Civilisation. It was flourished in the basins of the Indus river.
chapter: The people of Indus Valley Civilization

explanation: Shortugai – Afghanistan Mohenjo Daro – Pakistan Daimabad – Maharashtra, India Ganweriwala – Pakistan Diamabad is a deserted village and an archaeological site on the left bank of the Pravara river, a tributary of the Godavari river in Ahmednagar district of Maharashtra.

question: Staple crops of the Indus Valley people were
explanation: Their main staples were wheat and barley. Indus Civilization, also called Indus Valley Civilization or Harappan Civilization, the earliest known urban culture of the Indian subcontinent.

question: A well-designed sewage and drainage system were the characteristics of which of the following ancient civilizations?
explanation: The Harappan Culture was distinguished by its system of town planning. The well-designed sewage and drainage system of Mohenjo-daro was very impressive. In almost all cities every big or small house had bathroom. The street drains were equipped with manholes. Perhaps, no other Bronze Age Civilization gave so much attention to health and cleanliness as the Harappan did.
chapter: The people of Indus Valley Civilization

question: The primary profession of the Indus Valley Civilization was
explanation: The Indus Valley Civilization was an Ancient Civilization along the lower Indus river. The primary profession of the Indus Valley Civilization was agriculture. Agriculture in the Indus valley was solely depended on good quality soil and advancements in farming techniques major crops produced were Wheat, Barley Peas, Lentils, Linseed and Mustard Seed were sown in winter whereas Millet, Sesame and Rice were grown in summer.

question: Which of the following statements is/are correct regarding Indus Valley Civilization? 1. In Mohenjo-Daro the largest building is a granary. 2. Most impressive and unique feature of Dholavira is its water harvesting and management system. 3. Most distinctive feature of Lothal is the Dockyard
explanation: In reference to Indus Valley Civilization the most impressive and unique feature of Dholavira is its water harvesting and management system. In Mohenjo-daro the largest building was a granary. Dockyard which was part and most distinctive feature of Lothal.

question: Which of the following statements is/are correct about Harappan seals? 1. The script used in seals is Prakrit. 2. The script was written from right to left. 3. The seals were used as amulets for spiritual purposes.
explanation: Harappan seals were written from right to left and they were used as amulets for spiritual purposes. Long rectangular seals with no animal motifs from the last part of the Harappan phase found at Harappa and Mohenjodaro. This type of seal is found with only abstract writing, which radically altered communication. Impressions made by the square seals carry two distinct messages, one is presented in a script that could only have been understood by a literate person and the other in the animal motif, that even a child could comprehend. • The usual material for Harappan seals was steatite, a soft stone. • Quadrate type of seals was the most common in Harappan culture.
chapter: The people of Indus Valley Civilization

question: Who had discovered Mohenjodaro?
explanation: Rakhaldas Banerjee discovered Mohen- jodaro in 1922. He was an Indian archaeologist and museum expert. Note : Mohen-Jodaro is an archaeological site in the province of Larkana Sindh, Pakistan, built around 2500 BCE. S.R. Rao–He was an Indian archaeologist who led teams credited with discoveries of number of Harappan sites including the port city Lothal and Bet Dwarka in Gujrat. J.P. Joshi–He is an Indian archaeologist who discovered the Indus Vally sites of Dholovira and Surkotada. Joshi served as director general of the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) from 1987-1990.

question: Which one of the following animals was not represented on the seals and/or terracotta art of the Harappan culture?
explanation: From the seals, terracotta figures and copper tablets, we get an idea on the religious life of the Harappan. The chief male deity was Pashupati, represented in seals as sitting in a yogic posture with three faces and two horns. He was surrounded by four animals (elephant, Tiger, Rhino, and Buffalo is facing different direction) Two deer appear on his feet.

question: Which of the following statements is/are correct regarding the Indus valley civilization? 1. Mohenjo- daro is the largest site of Indus Valley Civilization. 2. Most impressive and unique feature of Dholavira is its water harvesting and management system. 3. Most distinctive feature of Lothal is the dockyard
explanation: Indus Civilization, also called Harappan Civilization, the earliest know urban culture of the Indian subcontinent. The nuclear dates of the civilization appear to be about 2500-1700 BCE, thought the southern sites may have lasted later into the 2nd millennium BCE. The Civilization was first identified in 1921 at Harappa in the Punjab region and then in 1922 at Mohenjo-Daro, near the Indus River in the Sindh region. • Mohenjo-Daro was discovered in 1922 by R.D. Banerji, an officer of the Archaeological Survey of India. Mohenjo-Daro was the largest site of the Indus Valley Civilization. It was designated a UNESCO World heritage site in 1980. • Dholavira was discovered by JP Joshi in 1967-68 and it is located in Gujarat near Rann of Kachchh. It is the fifth largest of eight major Harappan sites. • Dholavira shows excellent City planning, Wide Roads, Architecture with Geometric precisions and craftsmanship. More importantly they adopted very building series of connected reservoirs, stone and terracotta drainage pipes to wells. • Lothal is located between the Sabarmati river and its tributary Bhogavo, in the saurasthra region. The most distinctive feature of Lothal is the dockyard, which is on the eastern edge of the site.
chapter: The people of Indus Valley Civilization

question: The famous 'dancing-girl' statue of the Harappan Civilization was made using ____material
explanation: The famous "Dancing Girl" and seal of Pashupati found in Mohenjo -Daro (Harappan Civilization) about 2300-1750 BC. It is a prehistoric bronze sculpture made in low-wax casting. The 10.8 cm long bronze statue of the dancing girl was found in 1926 from a house in Mohenjo-daro
chapter: The people of Indus Valley Civilization

question: The most striking feature of the Indus Valley Civilization was
explanation: Indus Valley Civilization, also called Harappan Civilization, the earliest known urban culture of the Indian Subcontinent. The striking feature of the Indus Valley Civilization was Town Planning. •This civilization flourished around 2500 BC in Pakistan and Western India. • Some of the important Harappan Sites are – Harappa, Mohenjo-daro, Sutkagandor (Pakistan), Kalibangan (Rajasthan), Lothal and Surkotada (Gujarat), Banawali (Haryana), Dholavira (Gujarat) etc. • In 1924, John Marshall, Director-General of Archaeological Society of India (ASI), announced the discovery of a few civilizations in the Indus Valley to the world. • Indus Valley Civilization was the biggest of ancient uproar civilization of Egypt, Mesopotamia, India and China.

question: The earliest city to be discovered in the Indian history is :
explanation: The earliest city to be discovered in India was Harappa. It was excavated in 1920 in the Punjab province of British India. It was discovered by Daya Ram Sahni. • Mohanjo-daro-A piece of woven cotton along with spindle whorls and needles, a bronze figurine of a dancing girl has been found at Mohanjo-daro. It has also given evidence of violence leading to death. • Kalibangan is famous site of Indus Valley Civilization in Rajasthan. It is famous for pottery, black bangles. • Lothal is famous for the discovery of several ruins of Indus Valley Civilization. The site has been nominated to be enlisted as a UNESCO world Heritage site. • Ropar is the first Indus Valley excavation site in independent India.

explanation: The plough of Terracotta was found in the Harappan site of Banawali. Banawali site is situated in Fatehabad district, Haryana. explanation: Manda is situated on the right side of the bank of Chenab River in the foothills of Pir Panjal range, 28 km northwest of Jammu, and was considered the northernmost limit of the Harappan Civilization. It is considered the northernmost site of Indus Valley Civilization. • Indus Valley Civilization is also known as Harappan Civilization is well-known for its organized planning based on the grid system.

It is also known as Harappan Civilization. • The main crops of the Harappan Civilization are- Wheat, Barley, Peas, Sesamum, Millet, Cotton etc but Sugarcane was not cultivated in Harappa Civilization. Note:- Harappans used bronze (Copper + Tin), hence this civilization is also called Bronze Age Civilization.

explanation: The Great Bath was located in Ancient Indus Valley Civilization excavated at Mohenjodaro in Sindh, Pakistan.

question: The Indus Valley Civilization belongs to the
explanation: The Indus Valley Civilization, also called as Harappan Civilization belongs to Chalcolithic Age. The culture based on the use of copper and stone is termed as Chalcolithic, also called as stone-copper phase. The Chalcolithic or Copper Age in the transitional period between the Neolithic and the Bronze Age. ‘John Marshall’ was the first researcher to use the term, Indus Valley Civilization, (2500-1750BC). The capital cities are Mohenjodaro and Harappa. The Port cities are Sutkagendor, Balakot, Lothal, Allahdino and Kuntasi.

question: The Harappa site showing evidence of two cultural phases. Harappan and pre-Harappan, is
explanation: Kalibangan and Banwali, ancient site of the Indus Valley Civilization is located in northwestern India. The site contains both pre-Harappan and post Harappan remains, and therein can be seen the transition between the two cultures. Although the pre- Harappan culture used copper and produced pottery, it had no writing system, and its ruins lack the orderly layout and use of baked brick that is found in later Harappan sites.

question: The word 'Satyameva Jayate' inscribed below the Ashokan Lion Capital, the National Emblem of India, is taken from
explanation: Satyameva Jayate (Truth alone triumphs) is a part of a mantra from the Hindu scripture Mundaka Upanishad. • 26th It was adopted as the national motto of India on January 1950. • It is inscribed in the Devnagari script at the base of the Lion Capital of Ashoka and forms an integral part of the Indian National Emblem. 21 / 896 Rigveda : It is an ancient Indian religious book. question: Which of the Rigveda's Mandala is completely dedicated to Soma?
explanation: The ninth Mandala of the Rigveda also known as 'Soma Mandala'. • It has 114 hymns devoted to soma Pavamana "purifying soma" the sacred potion of the vedic religion. • The tenth Mandal of Rigveda related to Purusha Sukta from where Varna system is mentioned.

question: More than one thousand eulogies have been included in the Rigveda, what are they called?
explanation: Rigveda contains more than 1000 hymns and each hymn is called 'Sukta'. The term 'Sukta' means well said. These hymns are in praise of gods.

question: The Battle of Ten Kings in the Rig Vedic Age was fought on the river
explanation: The Dasarajna or Battle of the Ten Kings was a supposed conflict between various Aryan and Non- Aryan tribes in the region of Punjab. • The battle took place on the banks of the river Ravi (then Parusni). • The Battle of Ten Kings is a battle, first alluded to in the 7th mandala of the Rigveda, between a Bharata king and a confederation of tribes. It resulted in a decisive victory for the Bharatas and subsequent formation of the kuru polity.

question: Which of the following is not true about the ancient texts of India?

options.a: The Rig Veda is the earliest of all Vedic texts
options.b: The Rig Veda contains both prayers and rituals
options.d: Panini's writings illustrate the rules of Sanskrit grammar

explanation: Sacred hyms, which are a large body of texts originating in ancient India. Composed in Vedic Sanskrit, the text constitute the oldest layer of Sanskrit literature and the oldest scriptures of Hinduism. Later commentary on the Vedas, Brahmanas and Upnishads.

question: In which Veda there is Gayatri Mantra
explanation: The Gayatri Mantra, also known as the Savitri Mantra, is a highly revered mantra from the Rigveda, dedicated to the Vedic deity Savitri. The Gayatri Mantra is cited widely in Vedic and Post-Vedic texts, such as Bhagavad Gita, Harivamsa and Manusmriti.

question: In the Ramayana, where did Bharata worship the 'Khadau', or Sandals of Rama?
explanation: Bharata is a Hindu deity depicted in the Indian epic Ramayan. Bharata was the younger half brother of Rama. In Nandigrama, Bharata worship the 'Khadau', or Sandals of Rama. Ramayana holds Bharata as a symbol of Dharma and Idealism, Bharata is mostly worshipped in Kerala. According to the Ramayana, Rama is the seventh avatar of Vishnu while Lakshman, Bharata and Shatrughna are considered as part – incarnations of Sheshanga, Shankha and Sudarshan Chakra respectively. One of the only temples in India dedicated to him is the Sangharatna Temple in Kerala. Ekchakra :– Ekchakra is a small village, located 20 km. away from the town of Rampurhat in the Birbhum district of West Bengal. Within Hindu tradition, the five Pandavas from the epic, Mahabharata are described as staying in Ekchakra during their years in exile.

question: Oldest of the Vedas is
explanation: There are 4 Vedas – Rigveda, Samaveda, Yajur Veda and Atharva Veda. One of the best sources of Ancient Indian history is vedic literature. Rig Veda– The oldest Veda is the Rig Veda. It has 1028 hymns called 'Suktas' and is a collection of 10 books called ''Mandalas" 22 / 896 The feature of Rig Veda– 1. Rigvedic books 2 to 9 deal with cosmology and deities. 2. 1028 hymns deal with deities including and are attributed and dedicated to a sage Rishi. • Samveda is known as the veda of melodies and chants • Yajurveda stands to mean 'worship knowledge'. • Athrava Veda stands to mean a Tatpurusha compound of atharvan, an ancient sage and knowledge.

question: Which is the oldest language of India?
explanation: Sanskrit, the ancient language of India, which can be traced back to 2000 BC in its earliest written form. Tamil Language–Tamil language is the recognized as the oldest language in the world and it is the oldest language of the Dravidian family. Telugu– Telugu is a Dravidian language spoken by Telugu people predominantly living in the Indian States of Andhra Pradesh and Telangana. Malayalam– Malayalam is a Dravidian language spoken in the Indian state of Kerala and the Union territories of Lakshadweep and Puducherry by the Malayali people.

question: Which early Vedic God was considered to be the destroyer of forts?
explanation: Indra was the most powerful God of the early Vedic age. He was also known as Purandara and the destroyer of forts. He used to annihilate the 'Rakshasas' by deploying his 'Vajra' weapon. He is also the God of Rain.

question: Which is the oldest known Smriti?
explanation: Manu Smriti is considered to be the oldest 2nd Smriti. It is variously dated to be from the century 3rd BCE to century CE. It presents itself as a discourse given by Dharma topics as duties, rights, laws, conduct virtues and others. • The Manu smriti also Known as the Manav- Dharmashastra or laws of Manu. • Narad smrti is also part of Dharmashastra text. It was supposedly composed arourd 100 BCE. It bears a lot of similarity to Manu Smriti.

question: Kalinga's King Kharvela was associated with which of the following dynasty?
explanation: Kharavela was a king of Kalinga in present day Odisha, India. He ruled somewhere around first or second century BCE. He was associated with Mahameghavahana dynasty (which is also known as 'Chedi dynasty'). The main source of information a bout Kharavela is his rock-cut Hathigumpha inscription.

question: Who is the exponent of a Vaisheshika Darshan (philosophy)?
explanation: Vaisheshika Darshan was founded by 6th 2nd Kanada kashyapa a round the to century BC. Vaisheshika or vaisesika is one of the six orthodox schools of Hindu Philosphy from ancient India.

question: 'Aryavarta' referred to
explanation: Aryavarta (abode of the Aryas) Sanskrit pronunciation was a term for Northern parts of India. Indian subcontinent in the ancient Hindu Texts such as Dharmashastras and Sutras, referring to the area of the Indian Subcontinent settled by Indo-Aryan tribes and where Indo-Aryan religion and rituals predominated.

question: The battle of ten kings as referred to in the Rigveda, was fought on the bank of the river
explanation: The battle that was fought between Bharatas on one hand and the host of ten chiefs on the other was known as Battle of Ten Kings (Dasraja). The battle was fought on the river Parushni (Ravi), and it gave victory to Sudas.

question: Which of the following is one of the Aranyaka of Rig Veda?
explanation: Aranyakas describe and discuss rituals from various perspectives, but some include philosophical speculations for example, the Katha Aranyaka discusses rituals connected with the Pravargya. The Aitareya Aranyaka includes explanation of the Mahavrata ritual from ritualistic to symbolic meta- ritualistic points of view. Aranyakas are sometimes identified as Karma-Kanda (ritualistic action), while the Upanishads are identified as Gyan-Kanda knowledge/spirituality section.

question: Indian symbol Swastika is evolved from :
explanation: The word Swastika comes from Sanskrit romanized, Swastika meaning "conducive to well- being". In Hinduism, the right-facing symbol (4) is called Swastika symbolizing Surya ("Sun") prosperity and good luck, while the left - facing symbol ( ) is called Sauwastika, Symbolising night or tantric aspects of Kali. 23 / 896

question: The painted grey ware culture, which was contemporary to the later Vedic period is characterized by _____ 1. Use of iron 2. Brick structures 3. Coins 4. Agriculture Select the correct statement/statements using the codes given below:
explanation: The Painted Grey ware culture is an Iron Age Indian culture of the western Gangetic plain & the Ghaggar-Hakra valley in the Indian subcontinent, conventionally dated 1200 to 600-500 BCE. PGW culture is associated with village & town settlement, Domesticated horses, ivory-working & the advent of Iron Metallurgy. Note : The later Vedic texts had references to niksha (gold), suvarna, shatanama and pala, but scholars are of the opinion that these terms do not indicate a wide circulation of conventional coins.

question: Patanjali is well-known for the compilation of
explanation: • The Yogasutra of Patanjali is a collection of 196 Sanskrit sutras (aphorisms) on the theory and practice of Yoga. • The Panchatantra is an ancient Indian collection of interrelated animal fables in Sanskrit verse and prose, arranged with a frame story. It consist of five book. • The Yoga Sutras of Patanjali was the most translated ancient Indian text in the medieval era, having been translated into 40 Indian languages and two non- Indian languages (Old Javanese and Arabic). • Ayurveda is a natural system of medicine with historical roots in the Indian subcontinent. • Brahma sutra was written by Badarayana with Ved Vyas.

question: Fill in the Blanks : The month of January is named after__________.
explanation: There are 18 known Puranas. The Matsya Purana is one of the eighteenth major Puranas and among the oldest and better preserved in the Puranic genre of Sanskrit Literature in Hinduism.

question: The Ajanta Caves were built in :
explanation: Ajanta is a series of rock-cut caves in the Sahyadri ranges (Western Ghats) on Waghora river near Aurangabad in Maharashtra. (cid:1) The caves were developed in the period between 200 B.C. to 650 A.D. (cid:1) The caves were designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1983.

question: Which of the following was not known to the Rigvedic period?
explanation: The first mention of the Varna system was found in Purusha Suktam verse of the ancient Sanskrit Rig Veda. • Joint family system is the basic unit in the Rigvedic society was the patriarchal family which consisted of members belonging to three or four generations. • Agriculture was the main occupation of Rigvedic people. • Marriage system also found in Rigvedic period.

question: In which of the following Vedas the word "Itihasa" is mentioned?
explanation: Apart from mathematics, science, Ayurveda, sociology, agricultural science, history is also mentioned in the Atharva Veda. This gives information about 'Moksha'. It is also called ‘Brahma’ Veda. It has 5977 mantras. It was composed by Atharvan and Angiras Rishi.

question: Which Veda, deals with the science of medicine?
explanation: – Atharvaveda, collection of hymns and inchantations that forms part of the ancient sacred literature of India known as the Vedas. There are 4 Vedas in Hindu sacred literature– (1) Rigveda, (2) Samveda, (3) Yajurveda (4) Atharvaveda. Most ailments, both physical and mental, were attributed to malevolent spirits and cures consisted of rituals, charms, mantras, medicines and surgical intervention. 24 / 896

question: Which one of the following dynasties was ruling at the time of Alexander's invasion?
explanation: Nanda Dynasty was ruling at the time of Alexander’s invasion. • The Nanda Dynasty ruled in the northern part of the 4th 5th Indian subcontinent during and century BCE founded by Mahapadma Nanda. • The Maurya Dynasty was a geographically extensive Iron age historical power in South Asia based in Magadha, founded by Chandra Gupta Maurya in 322 BCE • The Shunga Dynasty was an ancient dynasty from Magadha that controlled areas of the central and eastern Indian subcontinent from around 184 to 75 BCE, founded by Pushya Mitra Shunga. • The Kanva Dynasty overthrew the Shunga dynasty in parts of eastern and central India, and ruled from 75 BCE to 30 BCE, founded by Vasudeva Kanva.

question: According to ancient Indian legends Surabhi was

options.b: A treasure in the form of a cow, churned from the cosmic ocean
options.d: An intoxicating drink drunk during religious ceremonies

explanation: Kamadhenu also known as Surabhi, is a divine bovine goddess described in Hinduism as the mother of all Cows. She is a miraculous “cow of plenty” who provides her/his owner whatever her desires and is often portrayed as the mother of other cattle as well as the eleven Rudras.

question: Consider the following statements: Assertion (A): Buddhism became very popular in India during the life time of its founder. Reason (R): Buddha preached in the language of the people and did not harp on the caste system Of these statements:
explanation: Mahavir (599BC-527BC), also known as 24th 'Vardhaman' was Thirthankar of Jainism. He was born in Bihar State of India. He revised the Jain Dharma with five moral teachings under broad headings of Non- Violence (Ahimsa), Truthfulness (Satya), Non-Stealing (Asteya), Control of senses (Brahmacharya), and Non-attachment (Aparigraha). He is not the founder of Jain religion. Rishabhanatha was the first Thirthankar of Jainism and also known as the founder of Jainism.

question: The system of philosophy with which the name of Kapila is prominently associate is
explanation: The system of philosophy Kapila is prominently associated with Samkhya philosophy. • Purva Mimamsa philosophy is the results as the whole of reality (Vipanam Rtam) written by Rishi Jamini. • Nyaya Philosophy is most significant contributions to India philosophy were systematic development of the theory of logic, methodology and its treatises on epistemology. • Uttara Mimamsa is the vedanta, one of most significant of all Indian philosophies.

question: The institution of gotra appeared in the

options.b: Period of the Manav Dharma Shastra
options.d: Period of the consolidation of the text of the Mahabharata

explanation: The institution of gotra appeared in the Later Vedic Period. It's literal meaning is ‘cow pen’ or the place where cattle belonging to whole clan is kept, but later time Gotra meaning changed to ‘descent from a common ancestor’.

question: Rigveda did not mention of

options.c: The term 'Hindu' was derived from an old
options.d: Hodu Persian word, used sixth- fifth centuries BCE. It refer to the region east of the river Sindhu (Indus). The Arab continued the Persian usage and called this region "al Hind" and its people "Hindi". Later the Turks referred to the people east of the Indus as "Hindu", their land as "Hindustan" and their language was "Hindavi"

explanation: Rigveda is the oldest of all four Vedas. Rigveda is a collection of hymns and prayers. Rigveda contains 10 Mandals and 1028 hymns. (cid:1) Medicines and magical technique did not mention of Rigveda. Medicines and magical is mention in the Atharva veda. 25 / 896 6th 4. Political Condition During Century BC Emergence of Mahajanpadas Magadh Empire 6th 72. Which old Persian word was used in the - 5th century BCE to refer to the area east of the Indus?

question: Which of the following is NOT a work of Kalidasa?
explanation: Kalidasa, was a Sanskrit poet and dramatist, probably the greatest Indian writer of any epoch. The six works identified as genuine are the dramas– • Abhijnanashakuntalam, • Vikramorvashi • Malavikagnimitra • The epic poems Raghuvamsha • Kumarasambhava • Meghaduta Kama Sutra is not a work of Kalidasa, it is a work of Maharishi Vatsyayana.

question: Digambara and Shvetambara were major sects of which religion ?
explanation: Digambara believes that in order to achieve Niravana one must renounce or liberation, one must renounce everything including clothes. Therefore Digambara monks are completely nude. • Svetambara believes that practice of nudity is not essential to attain liberation. Now ever nuns in both the sects wear white clothes. Digambara and Shvetambara are two sects of Jainism.

question: Who was the founder of Nanda Dynasty?
explanation: The Nanda Dynasty was ruled by Magadhas in northern India from 345-321 BCE. It was founded by Mahapadma Nanda also known as Ugrasena. • Mahapadma Nanda was succeeded by his eight sons. Dhana Nanda was the last one.

question: Who was called “Agrammes” or “Xandrames” by the Greek writers?
explanation: Dhananand was called 'Agrammes' or 'Xandrames' by the Greek writers. The name Agrammes is possibly a distorted from the Sanskrit Augrasainya (son or descendent of Ugrasena). According to Mahabodhivamsa Dhananand was the last ruler of Nanda Dynasty. Dhananand was killed by Chandragupta Maurya, the founder of Mauryan Empire.

question: Taxila was capital of which among the 16 Mahajanpadas?
explanation: Taxila was a capital of the Gandhara kingdom. Taxila was the great centre for education. In the Buddhist text 'Anguttar Nikaya' 16 large state called Mahajanapadas were described which were mostly situated north of the Vindhyas and extended from the north-east frontier to Bihar of these Magadha, Koshala, Vatsa and Avanti seems to have been considerably powerful. Mahajanpadas Capital (1) Kosala – Sravasti/Ayodhya (2) Kuru – Indraprastha (3) Vajji – Vaishali (4) Gandhara – Taxila (5) Anga – Champa (6) Magadha – Rajgir and Pataliputra (7) Asmaka – Potan

question: Which Buddhist scripture describes about 16 Mahajanapadas?
explanation: Buddhist scripture Anguttara Nikaya describes about 16 Mahajanpadas. While Sutta Pitaka describes about the Jataka stories, the stories of previous births of Buddha, and the collection of Buddhist teachings Vinaya Pitaka describes about rules and regulation and daily life of monks and nuns. Abhidhamma Pitaka describes about Buddhist philosophy which is in the form of Question and Answer.

question: Bimbisara belongs to which dynasty?
explanation: Bimbisara was the founder of Haryanka dynasty. ⇒ Shishunaga was the founder of Shishunaga dynasty. ⇒ Nanda Dynasty was founded by Mahapadmnanda after killing last Shishunaga ruler Nandivardhana. ⇒ Mauryan Empire was founded by Chandragupta Maurya.

question: In Mauryan Period the western part of Malwa was known as Awantika whose capital was Ujjain and eastern part was known as Akra whose capital was
explanation: Avanti was an ancient Indian Mahajanapada, roughly corresponded to the present day Malwa region. According to the Budhist texts, the Anguttara Nikaya, Avanti was one of the 16 Mahajanapada. The Janapada was divided into two parts by the Vindhyas, the Northern part had its capital at Ujjayini and the Southern part had its capital at Mahishmati. • But after the depreciation of Mahishmati, Vidisha had the privilege of becoming the capital of North Malwa.

question: Which ruler founded the capital at Pataliputra?
explanation: The ancient city of Pataliputra was founded in the 5th century BCE by Ajatashatru. In ancient India Ajatashatru of the Haryanka dynasty, was the king of Magadha. He had set up his capital in Rajgriha. After some year his son and successor Udayin, also known as Udaybhadra came into power, he shifted the capital from Rajgriha to Pataliputra. Hence Udayin is credited as the founder of the city of Pataliputra.

question: To whom Gautam Buddha gave his last sermon at Kushinagar?
explanation: Lord Buddha gave his last sermon at Kushinagar district of U.P. to Subhadda. Lord Buddha delivered his last sermon, attained Mahaparinirvana (Salvation) in 483 BC and was cremated at Rambhar Stupa. He speeched his first sermon at Sarnath. The Buddha was born into a noble family of the Kshatriyas in Lumbini in 563 BCE. He was called Siddhartha Guautam in his childhood.

question: Who is claimed to be the founder of Magadha Kingdom?
explanation: Brihadratha is claimed to be the founder of Magadha kingdom. Brihadratha was the initiator of the Brihadratha dynasty, the earliest ruling dynasty of Magadha.

question: In ancient India, the earliest capital of Magadh kingdom was at :
explanation: The core of the Magadh Kingdom was the area of Bihar south of the Ganges. Its first capital was Rajagriha (Modern day Rajgir), then Patliputra (Modern Patna).

question: Match List-I with List-II to identify countries and select the correct answer using the codes given below the Lists : List-I List-II (Mahajanapada) (Ruler) A. Magadh 1. Prodyota B. Kosala 2. Prasenjit C. Avanti 3. Udayana D. Vatsa 4. Bimbisara Codes : A B C D
explanation: The correct sequence is- List I List II (Mahajanapada) (Ruler) A. Magadh Bimbisara B. Kosala Prasenjit C. Avanti Prodyota D. Vatsa Udayana

question: The first Tirthankara of Jain religion was
explanation: Jainism is an ancient religion that came into 6th prominence in century B.C. There were 24 great teachers who were called Tirthankaras. The first Tirthankara was Rishabhanatha. Lord Rishabhdev was the first Tirthankara of Jainism. He was born at Ayodhya in the Ikshvaku kula or clan. In Hinduism he is known to be an avatara or incarnation of Vishnu. The name of Rishabh's parents has been mentioned in the Bhagvata Purana. (cid:1) Mahavira was the 24th Tirthankara of Jainism. (cid:1) 23rd Parshvanatha was the Tirthankara. (cid:1) Neminath was the 22nd Tirthankara.

question: Who was the twenty-fourth Jain Tirthankara?
explanation: Mahavira was the twenty-fourth and last Jain Tirthankara ( 599-527 BC). • Rishabhnatha is the first Jain Tirthankara. • Mahavira also known as Vardhamana, taught that observance of the vows of Ahimsa (Non- violence), Satya (truth), Asteya (Non-stealing), Brahmacharya (Chastity) and Aparigraha (Non- Attachment) are necessary for spiritual liberation. 27 / 896

question: Buddha preached first sermon at
explanation: A Popular subject in Medieval Buddhist art is the Buddha preaching his first sermon in a deer forest at Sarnath, north of Bodh gaya, where he had experienced enlightment some weeks prior.

question: In which language did most Buddhist texts were written?
explanation: Most Buddhist texts were written in Pali language. Note:- Tripitaka were written in Pali and Tripitaka includes Vinaya pitaka, Sutta pitaka and Abhidhammn Pitaka. (cid:1) After 4th Buddhist council (held in kundalavan, Kashmir) Buddhist literature were started in sanskrit.

question: Which of the following statements is correct about the fourth Buddhist council?

options.a: Fourth Buddhist Council was held in Pataliputra
options.b: Fourth Buddhist Council was held in Rajgriha and patronized by Ajatasatru
options.c: Fourth Buddhist Council was held in Kashmir and patronized by Kanishka
options.d: First Buddhist Council was held in Kashmir and patronized by Ajatasatru

explanation: The fourth Buddhist Council was held in 72 AD at Kundalvana, Kashmir. It was presided by Vasumitra, while Asvaghosa was his deputy.

question: Which of the following statements is/are correct regarding Jainism? 1. The Jains believe in the Vedas 2. They admit the existence of a soul 3. There were twenty-four Tirthankaras, who actually established the Jaina darshan.
explanation: Jainism is an ancient religion from India. Jainism was born in India about the same period as Buddhism. It was established by Mahavira (599–527 BC). Jainism is a religion of self help. There are no Gods or spiritual beings that will help human beings. The first Tirthankara was Rishabhdev.

question: KalaChakra ceremony is associated with which of the following?
explanation: KalaChakra is a polysemic term in Vajrayana Buddhism that means “wheel of time” or “time cycle”. KalaChakra is also the name of a Series of Buddhist texts and major practice lineage in Indian Buddhism and Tibetan Buddhism.

question: Which Buddhist monk converted milinda (Indo-Greek king) to Buddhism?
explanation: • The Indo-Greek king, Milinda (or Menander) was converted to Buddhism by Nagasena, a Buddhist monk and philosopher. Before becoming a Buddhist, Menander asked Nagasena many questions relating to Buddhism. These questions and Nagasena's answers are recorded in a form of book known as ‘Milindapanho (or The questions of Milinda). • Gautama Buddha, popularly known as Lord Buddha or the Buddha, was an ascetic, a religious leader and teacher who lived in ancient India. He is also called as Shakyamuni and Light of Asia.

question: Which among the following books was written by Jain monk Hemachandra?
explanation: Acharya Hemachandra was an India Jain Scholar, Poet, Mathematician and Polymath who wrote on Grammer, Philosophy, Prosody, Mathematics and Contemporary history. To illustrate the grammer, he produced the epic poetry Dvyashraya kavya on the history of Chalukya Dynasty.

question: Where was the first Sikh Guru, Guru Nanak Dev, born in 1469?
explanation: Guru Nanak Dev:- He was the first guru of the Sikhs. He was born in 1469 at a place called Talwandi (present day Nankana Sahib Pakistan). His father was Mehta Kalu Chandra Khatri and mother's name was Tripta Devi. Guru Nanak was a Philosopher, Yogi religious reformer and social reformer and was a staunch opponent of superstition and external ostracism. He died on 22 Sep. 1539 in Kartarpur, Pakistan.

question: Jambudvipa in 14th century Java's text was refe rred t o
explanation: A Jambudvipa is the Dvipa (island or continent) of the terrestrial world as envisioned in the cosmologies of Hinduism. 14th In century in Java Scripts Jambudvipa was used to refer to that place where "blackberry" was found in a great number.

question: Who founded Ajivika sect?
explanation: Aajivika are followers of the doctrine of immutability or pre-determined belonging to the religious order or sect founded by Gosala Makkaliputta, a senior contemporary of Buddha and Mahavira. The basic theme of Ajivikism is the doctrine of Niyati or destiny. The main source of information on Ajivikism is Bhagvati Sutra. 28 / 896

question: Which discourse of Gautama Buddha has been called as the "Discourse on the Turning of the wheel of Law"?

options.a: Discourse at the Deer park at Sarnath

explanation: The Buddha's first discourse is called the Dhammachakkappavattana Sutta (The discourse of the turning of the wheel of Dhamma). The Buddha gave this discourse to his five former companions at the Deer Park at Sarnath.

question: The first country in which Buddhism was propagated outside India is
explanation: Various rulers from different dynasty gave royal patronage to Buddhism. Due to royal patronage and it's simplicity this religion became popular in different countries. King Ashoka and King Kanishka send different missionaries to different countries like Sri Lanka, Greece and Syria etc. Sri Lanka earlier known as Ceylon was the first country outside to India where Buddhism was propagated and adopted.

question: Buddhism was divided into Mahayana and Hinayana during the reign of which of the following rulers?
explanation: Kanishka I, or Kanishka the Great was an emperor of the Kushan dynasty in the second century. He was famous for his military, political and spiritual achievements. He was also a liberal patron of Indian art and literature. During the reign of Kanishka, the Buddhism was divided into Mahayan & Hinayana. • Hinayana:–Follows the Buddha's teachings exactly. • Mahayana:–They believe that individual interpretation is important.

question: Where was Guru Govind Singh educated and learnt Persian?
explanation: Govind Rai, whose name was altered to Govind Singh possibly at the time of the creation of the Khalsa, was born in Patna, the only child of Guru Tegh Bahadur. At the age of five he was brought to Anandpur and educated in Sanskrit and Persion.

question: Who was the founder of Bahmani Kingdom?
explanation: In Deccan (South India), the Bahmani kingdom had come into existence in 1347, the later period of Muhammad Bin Tughlaq, by an Afghan adventurer Gangu who assumed the title of Alauddin Hasan Bahaman Shah. He made Gulburga the capital of newly established empire and renamed it Ahsanabad. Later in 1425 Bidar became its capital.

question: Which of the following element is common in both Buddhism and Jainism Philosophy?
explanation: Mahavira and Buddha founded Jainism and Buddhism respectively. Both believed that Nirvana or Salvation is the liberation from eternal chain of birth and death.

question: Which of the two Jain Tirthankaras are mentioned in the Rig Veda?
explanation: The names of two Jain Tirthankaras (1st Rishabhdev Tirthankara of Jainism is known to be an incarnation of Lord Vishnu) and Arishtanemi (22nd Tirthankara also called Neminatha) are mentioned in the Rigveda. (cid:1) Arishtanemi is considered to be a close relative of Lord Krishna. The symbol of Rishabhdev is Taurus. He was the first Tirthankara of Jainism and Non-Violence was his motto.

question: In Gandhara School of Art, the image of which of the following deities is a prominent feature?
explanation: In the Gandhara art the image of Buddha were made in the Greco-Roman style which is a synthesis of Greek, Roman and Indian features that are reflected in the image of Buddha.

question: Select the correct sequence of places associated with the major events in the life of Buddha :
explanation: Lumbini was the birth place of Lord Buddha. – Bodhgaya was the place where he got his enlightenment. – Sarnath is where he delivered his first sermons after the enlightenment. – Kushinagar was the death place of Lord Buddha.

question: Consider the following reason and assertion and choose answer from the codes given below A: Jainism came into prominence in the 6th century B.C 6th R : century B.C. was an age of intellectual and philosophical upsurge

options.a: (A) is correct (R) is a correct explanation for it
options.b: (A) and (R) both are correct but (R) is not correct explanation

explanation: Jainism came into prominence in the 6th century B.C., when lord Mahavira propagated the religion. There were 24 great teachers the last of whom was Lord Mahavira. These 24 teachers were called Tirthankaras.

question: In which of the following villages the Ancient Buddhist Stupa is located
explanation: The state of Telangana is home to many religious sites of historical and heritage prominence. One such renowned site for Buddhism is Dhulikatta village in Peddapalli District. The Buddhist circuit of the state has many prominent places that spell their glory. One such prominent place of Buddhism takes Nestting in Telangana at Dhulikatta. The village is only 25 km away from Kareemnagar. The Buddhist mahastupa or great stupa and Vihar in 2nd Dhulikatta village date back to century BC, which is about 2000 years ago.

question: Match List-I with List-II and select the correct answer using the codes given below- List-I List-II
explanation: Matching the List-I with List-II Digambaras → Orthodox and conservative Jainism. Svetambaras → Unorthodox and Liberal Jainism. Terapanthis→Breakaway group from the Swetambaras → Somaiyas Breakaway group from the Digambaras.

question: The 24th Thirthankara of Jainism was :
explanation: The 24th and last Tirthankara of Jainism was Mahavir Swami (599 BC-527 BC) • The First Tirthankara of Jainism was Rishabhdeva • The 23rd Tirthankara of Jainism was Parshwanatha. Note : Asvaghosha was a Buddhist philosopher, poet and orator. He is belived to have been the first sanskrit dramatist and is considered the greatest Indian poet prior to Kalidasa.

question: Consider five great events of Buddha's life and their symbols. Identify which are correctly matched. Five Great Events Symbols (i) Birth - Horse (ii) Great renunciation - Lotus and bull (iii) Nirvana - Bodhi Tree (iv) First Sermon - Dharmachakra or wheel (v) Parinirvana - Stupa
explanation: Five great Event Symbol (cid:1) → Buddha's Birth Lotus & Bull (cid:1) → Great renunciation or Horse The Great Departure (Mahabhinishkramana) (cid:1) → Nirvana (Enlightment) Bodhi tree (cid:1) → First Sermon (Dhammachakara Pravartan) (cid:1) Death (Parinirvana) → Stupa

question: Which of the following style is called Greece- Buddhist art style ?
explanation: Gandhar style is called Greece - Buddhist art style. Many of the stylistic elements in the represent actions of the Buddha point to Greek influence are: (1) Himation (2) The contrapposto stance of the upright figures. (3) The stylized curly hair (4) The origins of Greece - Buddhist art are to found in the Hellenistic.

question: Gautam Buddha was born at –
explanation: Gautam Buddha was born as Siddhartha Gautam in 563 BCE, in a royal family in Lumbini. Which is situated near the Indo-Nepal border. His family belonged to the sakya clan which ruled from Kapilvastu, Lumbini. Note:- Gautam Buddha died at the age of 80 in 483 BCE at Kushinagar, Uttar Pradesh. The event is known as Mahaparinirvana.

question: Which philosophy is associated with the Mahayana Buddhism :
explanation: Mahayana Buddhism believe that the right path of a follower will lead to the redemption of all human beings. The Hinayana believe that each person is responsible for his own fate. Along with these doctrines there are other Buddhist belief like 'Zen Buddhism ' from Japan and the 'Hindu tantric Buddhism' from Tibet. Madhyamaka or Shunyavada and Yogacara are the two main philosophical trajectories associated with the Mahayan stream of Buddhist thought.

question: 'Shravanabelagola' is associated with
explanation: Shravanabelagola is a town located near Channarayapatna of Hassan district in the Indian state of Karnataka. • It is associated with Jainism. It is one of the most important Tirthas in Jainism. • Chandragupta Maurya is said to have died here in 298BCE.

question: Who among the following recommended attaining eight fold path of salvation?
explanation: The Eight fold path of Buddhism, also called the middle path or middle way is the system of following these eight divisions of the path to achieve spiritual enlightenment and cease suffering. (1) Right understanding, (2) Right thought, (3) Right speech, (4) Right Action, (5) Right Livelihood (6) Right effort (7) Right Mindfulness, (8) Right concentration (Meditation)

question: The first human statues worshipped in India were those of
explanation: The first human idol that was worshipped in India was of Buddha. The fourth Buddhist Council was organized in Kundalvan, Kashmir in the first century AD, in which the Buddhism was divided into Hinayana and Mahayana. Hinayana considers Buddha's Atma Dipo Bhava as his original, while Mahayana worships Buddha as a deity. Although the idol worship started from the Indus Valley Civilization itself but Buddha was first worshiped as a human god.

question: Where is the ''Nirwan Sthala'' (death place) of Buddha?
explanation: Gautam Buddha "Nirwan Sthala" located in Kushinagar. • He is also called Shakyamuni Buddha because he was a member of the Sakya clan. • After Buddha attained enlightenment at Bodh Gaya in 528 BC, he came to Sarnath and preached first sermon.

question: Which event of Buddha's life is represented by "wheel with deer" in India art?
explanation: The first preaching event of Gautam Buddha's life is represented by the wheel with deer in Indian art. Mahatama Buddha gave the first preaching or sermon at Mrigadava Sarnath.

question: The jains believe that Mahavira was the last of the
explanation: Ashoka's Dharma represented the principles of Buddhism. Dharma or Dhamma is a set of edicts that formed the policy of Ashoka (Mauryan Emperor).

question: Where is Bodh Gaya located from where the Prince Gautam attained enlightenment and became Lord Buddha?
explanation: Bodh Gaya is a religious site and place of pilgrimage associated with the Mahabodhi Temple complex in Gaya district in the Indian state of Bihar. It is famous as Gautam Buddha attained enlightenment under Bodhi tree. Bodh Gaya is the most important of the main four pilgrimage sites related to the life of Gautam Buddha, the other three being Kushinagar, Lumbini and Sarnath. Mahabodhi Temple, located in Bodh Gaya, became a UNESCO world Heritage site in 2002.

question: Ambapali whose name occurs in early Buddhist literature was

options.d: The author of the work Buddhacharita

explanation: Ambapali whose name occurs in early Buddhist literature was a courtesan of Vaishali in ancient India around 500 BC. 31 / 896

question: Which one of the following is a doctrine said to have been added by Mahavira to the four other doctrines propounded by previous teachers?
explanation: Observe Brahamacharya is a doctrine said to have been added by Mahavira to the four other doctrines propounded by previous teachers. The five vows of Mahavira's teachings are- (i) Ahimsa – Non-violence (ii) Asteya – Non-stealing (iii) Satya – Truth (iv) Aparigraha – Non-attachment (v) Brahmacharya – Chastity

question: Megasthenes was at the court of
explanation: Megasthenes was an Greek ambassador for seleucid king Seleucus-I Nicator and to the court of king Chandragupta Maurya in Pataliputra (Modern Patna, Bihar). In his book Indica he has given a detailed account of Mauryan India.

question: The "Arthashastra" was written by :
explanation: Arthashastra was written by Chanakya. It is an Ancient Indian treatise on statecraft economic policy and military strategy. Kautilya also identified as Vishnugupta and Chanakya, is traditionally credited as the author of the text. Kautilya was the Prime Minister of Chandragupta Maurya.

question: Which of the following officials was entrusted with the collection of taxes in the Arthshastra
explanation: Sannidhata was entrusted with the collection of taxes in Arthashastra of Kautilya.

question: Who among the following was the first ruler to inscribe his message to his subjects and officials on stone surfaces, natural rocks and polished pillars?
explanation: The Stone surface on which Ashokan inscriptions were written, included the natural rocks as well as the polished pillars. Ashoka used the inscriptions to Proclaim Dhamma of Buddhism. Ashoka was the first ruler to inscribe his message to his subject and officials on stone surface, natural rocks and polished pillars. Note- The first success in reading the inscription described on this inscription came to James Prinsep (in 1873.)

question: Gautam Buddha's teachings are found mainly in:
explanation: In the Separate Kalinga Edicts Ashoka also enunciated his famous doctrine of administration i.e. "All men are my children". As I desire for my children that they all should enjoy bliss and happiness in this world and next, the same I desire also for all my men”

question: Amitraghata/Slayer of foes referred in Greek sources, is
explanation: The son of the Maurya dynasty’s founder Chandragupta Maurya and also the second Mauryan Emperor of India, Bindusara (Born c. 320BCE-died 272BCE–throne about 297BCE) was famously known as Amitraghata (or Amitrochates) by the Greeks, which in Sanskrit means the slayer of enemies. Bindusara was the father of the great Indian Emperor Ashoka.

question: Under the influence which Buddhist monk, did Ashoka become an ardent supporter of Buddhism?
explanation: Ashoka has been particularly influenced by the famous Buddhist monk Upagupta. Due to his influence Ashoka embraced Buddhism. Upagupta is also known as the spiritual teacher of Mauryan king Ashoka. Upagupta’s guru was the disciple of Buddha’s attendant Ananda and his name was Sanavasi.

question: Under Mauryan rule, the royal highway that encouraged trade was from ........... .
explanation: Under Mauryan rule, the royal highway that encouraged trade, was from Pataliputra to Taxila. Chandragupta Maurya of the Mauryan Empire in ancient India built this highway along the ancient route called Uttarapatha in 3rd century BCE.

question: Which of the following rulers erected his decrees at public places?
explanation: Ashoka expanded the borders and influence of the empire to its height. As he did, his architectural influence spread across India. When Ashoka passed an imperial decrees, he often had it carved into sandstone pillars that were erected across the empire. These pillars are the oldest examples of monumental sculpture in India with more than 40 found across the subcontinent.

question: The religious sect Ajivika, at the time of Mauryas was founded by
explanation: Ajivika, an ascetic sect that emerged in India about the same time as Buddhism and Jainism 14th and that lasted untill the century. The name may mean "following the ascetic way of life." It was founded by Goshala Maskariputra (also called Gosala 24th Makkhaliputta) a friend of Mahavira, the Tirthankara of Jainism. His doctrines and those of his followers are known only from Buddhist and Jain sources, which state that he was lowborn and died after a quarrel with Mahavira shortly before in Buddha died.

question: Kautilya's Arthashastra is a book on
explanation: Chanakya has been traditionally identified as Kautilya or Vishnugupta. He dedicated his life to forming the Maurya Empire and guiding its pioneer Chandragupta Maurya and his son, Bindusara. He wrote a book Arthashastra, that means ‘The Science of Material Gain’ is the philosopher’s guide to running an empire.

question: Which of the following was not adopted from the Maurya Dynasty in the emblem of Government of India?
explanation: The emblem of the Government of India is adopted from the Lion Capital of Ashoka at Sarnath as a state emblem. This was officially adopted on 26 January

question: The actual Sarnath Capital features four lions standing back to back, symbolizing power, courage, confidence and pride, mounted on a circular base. • The integral part of the emblem is their motto which is inscribed below the abacus in Devanagari script. • Satyameva Jayate which is quoted from the Mundaka Upanishad, the sacred part of Hindu Vedas. 139. The last Mauryan king was
explanation: In 322 B.C., Chandra Gupta Maurya with the help of Chanakya founded the Mauryan Dynasty. • Megasthenes was the Greek ambassador at Chandragupta's court. • Brihadratha was the last ruler of the Maurya Empire. He was assassinated by his general, Pushyamitra Shunga, who went on to establish the Shunga Empire.

question: With whom of the following King of Byzantine (Syria) the Mauryan Emperor Bindusara had established friendly relations ?
explanation: Mauryan Emperor Bindusara had friendly relations with the Greek King Antiochus-I of Byzantine (Syria). Deimachus was a Syrian ambassador who came in the court of Bindusara. Mauryan Emperor Ashoka the Great : In his rock edict 13th mentions the names of 5 Hellenic kings – Antiochus II of Syria, Ptolemy II of Egypt. Antigonus of Messedonia, Mages of Syria, Alexander of Epirus. He sent missionaries to all of them. In 305 BC, Chandra Gupta Maurya defeated Selucas Niketar and received the territories of Kabul and Baluchistan.

question: Ashoka's major edict is found in one of the following places in Karnataka. Identify it.
explanation: The Major Rock Edicts of Indian Emperor Ashoka refers to 14 separate major edicts of Ashoka which are significantly detailed & represent some of the earliest dated rock inscriptions of any Indian Monarch. There are total 14 major edicts of Ashoka which are located in India, Pakistan and Afghanistan. Major Rock Edicts of Ashoka : Kandahar, Yerragudi, Girnar, Dhauli, Jaugada, Khalsi, Sopara, Shahbazgarhi, Mansehra, Sannati. Note : Brahmagiri, Hampi and Nittur edicts are minor rock edicts of Ashoka found in Andhra Pradesh.

question: Which of the following inscriptions of Ashoka, indicates the principle of religious tolerance?
explanation: • Rock Edict XII– Directed and determind request for tolerance among different rellgious sects. • Rock Edict X– Condemns the desire for fame and glory. Stresses on the popularity of Dhamma. • Pillar Edict VII– Work done by Ashoka for Dhamma policy. He says that all sects desire both self-control and purity of mind.

question: The first king of Mauryan dynasty was -
explanation: The first king of Mauryan Dynasty was Chandra Gupta. The Mauryan Empire was founded in 321 BCE by Chandragupta a Mauryan king who had overthrown the Nanda Dynasty. • Chronological order of Mauryan King – Chandragupta → Bindusara → Ashoka • Bimbisara was the founder of Haryak Dynasty.

question: From which place of Uttarakhand the Ashokan rock edict is reported?
explanation: Famous Mauryan Emperor, Ashoka's inscriptions as rock edicts is present in Khalsi (Uttarakhand). The site of Ashoka's inscriptions at Khalsi is singularly unique as it is the one place in North India, where the great Mauryan Emperor has inscribed the set of the fourteen rock edicts. The language of these edicts is "Prakrit" and the script is of Brahmi. James Prinsep, a British antiquary and colonial administrator was the first person to decipher Ashoka's edicts.

question: In which dynasty the 'decimal system' was introduced?
explanation: The decimal numeral system, including the concept of zero was invented in India during the Gupta Dynasty. Brahmagupta a scholar and mathematician in AD 628, first time defined zero. • The Gupta dynasty established by Shri Gupta/Ghatotkaksha. • Chandra Gupta I assumed the title of Maharajadhiraja. • Kumar Gupta I founded Nalanda University.

question: Where did Chandragupta Maurya spend his last days?
explanation: Shravanabelagola - When the famine came in Magadha Chandragupta made efforts to counter it but, dejected by the tragic conditions prevailing. He left to spend his last days in the service of Bhadrabahu at Shravanbelagola, a famous religious site in South Western India. Where Chandragupta fasted to death or 'died of starvation' known as sallekhana method also.

question: Whose envoy was Megasthenes?
explanation: Megasthenes was an ancient Greek historian and diplomat. He became an ambassador of Seleucus Nicator to Chandragupta Maurya in Pataliputra. Who wrote a book called Indica which is an account of Mauryan India. For his pioneering work he is regarded as the ‘Father of Indian History’.

question: Chandragupta Maurya built the first empire in with the help of
explanation: Chandragupta Maurya was the first emperor of the Maurya Empire. He defeated the Nandas (Dhana Nanda) and founded Mauryan Dynasty in 321 BC (in some books may be 320 BC) with the help of Kautilya and he had overthrown Nanda dynasty and rapidly expanded his power westward across central and western India in order to take advantage of the descriptions of local powers in the wake of the withdrawl by Alexander the great's army.

question: In ancient and early medieval India Agrahara denoted

options.c: Tax free land gifted to temples
options.d: Tax free village possessed by Brahmans

explanation: Agrahara denoted as tax free village possessed by Brahmans in ancient and early medieval India. Typically it's granted by a king or a noble family in India for religious purposes particularly to Brahmins to maintain temples in that land or a pilgrimage site and to sustain their families.

question: Which one of the following mentioned division of the Mauryan Society into seven classes?
explanation: The division of Mauryan society into seven classes was particularly mentioned in 'Indica' of Megasthenes. These seven classes are :–

question: The wise men (Brahmins) 2. Farmers 3. Herdsmen 4. Artisans 5. Soldiers 6. Over lookers 7. Government officials including army and navy officers. 151. In which one of the following do we come across a detailed account of the municipal administration of Mauryas?
explanation: The account of Megasthenes named ‘Indica’ is a detailed account of the municipal administration of Mauryas. Megasthenes was the ambassador of Seleucus Nicator in the court of Chandragupta Maurya. The Arthashastra of Kautilya is an ancient Indian Sanskrit treatise on statecraft, economic policy and military strategy. In the Mudrarakshasa Play, Vishakhadatta narrates the ascent of the king Chandragupta Maurya. 34 / 896

question: Ashoka sent missionaries to
explanation: Ashoka was the third ruler of the Maurya Dynasty and ruled almost the entire Indian subcontinent from 268 to 232 BCE. He made Buddhism the state religion around 260 B.C. He sent missionaries too far of places such as Ceylon (Sri Lanka), Egypt, Macedonia, Tibet etc, to propagate the ideals of Buddhism and inspire people to live by the teachings of Lord Buddha.

question: Which one of the following is an example of Mauryan art?

options.a: Image of Nara-Narayana in the temple at Deogath
options.c: The panel showing Mahakapi Jataka at Bharhut

explanation: Bull capital, Rampurva is the example of Mauryan art, discovered in 1876, it is currently housed in Rastrapati Bhavan. Mauryan period in Indian subcontinent represented an important transition in the art and architectural form from the usages of wood to the stone. Mauryans ruled between 322 and 185 BCE and during this period many developments took place in the art and architecture leading to the establishment of an altogether a unique culture in the subcontinent.

question: Which of the following is not true about Charaka?

options.a: He was an expert on medicine
options.b: His writing is like an encyclopedia of Indian medicine
options.c: His book contains the names of a large number of plants and herbs of the time
options.d: He mentioned as many as 121 implements used in surgery

explanation: Charaka 300 BCE was one of the principal contributors to Ayurveda, a system of medicine and lifestyle developed in Ancient India. He is known as an editor of the medical treatise entitled Charaka Samhita, one of the foundational texts of classical Indian medicine and Ayurveda included under Brahat-Trayi.

question: Which one of the following books of ancient India has love story of the son of the founder of Sunga dynasty?
explanation: Malavikagnimitra also known as Malavika and Agnimitra was a Sanskrit play written by Playwright Kalidasa. The Book tells the love story of Agnimitra and Malavika and it is a five act play. Agnimitra was the second king of the Shunga dynasty. He succeeded his father, Pushyamitra Shunga in 149 BCE. Swapnavasavadatta is a Sanskrit play of six acts written by Bhasa. Meghadoota is a Sanskrit poem by Kalidasa. Ratanavali is a Sanskrit drama about a princess Ratnavali and a king Udayana written by Harshavardhana.

question: Which one of the following is NOT a title held by Gautamiputra Shatakarni?
explanation: Gautamiputra Satakarni is considered the greatest king of the Satavahana dynasty did not take "Chhatrapati" title whereas other titles had taken by him. Chhatrapati title was taken by Shivaji.

question: 'Kolikas' were the one of the important artisanal Sudra community during Satavahana period. What was their occupation?
explanation: During Satavahana period, the people were familiar with the Vedic fourfold division of society into Brahmins, Kshatriyas, Vaisyas and Sudras. Outside the four fold influence were the indigenous tribes with independent ways of life and thought. People were known according to their professions such as the Kolika (Weaver), the Sethi (Merchant), the Gadhika (Druggist), Halika (Cultivator), Kularika (Potters), Vasakara (Bamboo Worker) and Dhanntka (Com Dealar).

question: Who was the founder of the Shunga dynasty?
explanation: The Shunga Dynasty was founded by Pushyamitra Shunga after the fall of the Mauryan Empire in 185 BCE. Pushyamitra Shunga was also credited to repulse the two attacks of Greeks and also conquest over Vidarbha. There were ten Shunga rulers, four Great rulers of Shunga or Sunga Dynasty were Pushyamitra Sunga (185 BCE - 151 BCE), Agnimitra (149 BCE -141 BCE), Bhagabhadra (94 BCE -83 BCE) and Devabhuti (87 BCE-73 BCE). (cid:1) The last of the Shunga emperors was Devabhuti. He was assassinated by his minister Vasudeva Kanva.

question: _______ fought against King Han Ho-ti, who was the king of Han dynasty of China, and defeated him in the second attempt.
explanation: ‘Kanishka’ was the most powerful ruler of the ‘Kushana Empire’. The capital of his empire was Purushpura (Peshawar). Kanishka had fought against king Han Ho-ti who was the king of Han dynasty of China. Kanishka defeated the chinese king in the second attempt. Kanishka was the founder of the Shaka Era of A.D. 78.

question: For which of the following rulers 'Ekabrahmana' has been used ?
explanation: Gautamiputra Shatkarni was the greatest ruler of the Satavahana Empire (located in present Western Deccan). He is considered to be the destroyer of Palavas, Shakas & Yavanas. • He had the titles of Ekbrahmana, Trisamudradisvara & Agamanilaya.

question: Megasthenes was a Greek ambassador sent by :
explanation: Megasthenes, was a Greek ambassador and sent by Seleucus I Nicator in the court of King Chandragupta Maurya (Pataliputra). Indica is the reputed work of Megasthenes.

question: During the reign of Harsha, which educational centre was most famous?

Kumar Gupta I founded Nalanda University : – Taxila University established around 2700 years ago. – Vikramshila University was established by Dharmapala of Pala dynasty. – It was located in the Bhagalpur district of Bihar.

question: Which one of the following places has yielded earliest stupa railing ?
explanation: The “Great Stupa” at Sanchi is the oldest structure and was originally commissioned by the emperor Ashoka the great of the Maurya Empire in the 3rd Century BCE. (cid:1) Bharhut Stupa (Madhya Pradesh) was built at 3rd around century and was later improvised by the Sungas around 100 BCE. (cid:1) Amaravati Stupa (Andhra Pradesh) was built at around 250 BCE. (cid:1) 3rd Nagarjuna Stupa (Andhra Pradesh) was built in century AD, is located in Nagarjunkonda, Guntur.

question: According to historical evidence, which king renovated the Sudarshan Lake located in the state of Gujarat before its renovation during Gupta period?
explanation: Rudradaman-I was the most famous Shaka ruler. He ruled from 130 AD to 150 AD. His kingdom covered areas such as Konkan, Narmada valley, Malwa, other part of Gujarat and Kathiawar. His repaired work to improve the Sudarshana Lake in Kathiawar and Issued the first long inscription in Sanskrit.

question: Who introduced gold coins in India on a mass scale?
explanation: Kushan ruler, Vima Kadaphises (2nd Kadaphises), father of Kanishka, was the first one to issue gold coin in Roman Pattern and at a large scale around 100 CE. The gold coins were minted with punch marks and many coins contained the symbol of a bull or a Swastika. • Kushanas issued mostly Gold coins and numerous Copper coins which are found in most part of North India up to Bihar. • Many Indian Gods and Goddesses were depicted on Kushana coins besides many Persian and Greek deities. • The study of coins is called Numismatics.

question: Where did Vasco da Gama arrive in India in 1498?
explanation: 'Vasco da Gama' the first Portuguese and the first European traveller landed at Calicut, India with a Gujarati pilot Abdul Majid who had guided the ships from the African coast to Calicut. With the economic revival of Europe, the demand for the spices and drugs from the India and South East Asia, especially the pepper and spices which makes meat more tasty, increases. Vasco da Gama profited 60 times higher with the trade of Indian pepper which encourages other Portuguese too. Pedro Alvares Cabral was the second traveler who came to India. (1500 A.D.) The first Portuguese viceroy of India was Francisco de Almeida and the Portuguese empire in Indian coast was known as Estado da India. 36 / 896

question: Which of the following is not true about the Sangam literature?

options.c: Its final compilation may have been completed by the sixth century A.D.
options.d: The chiefs and kings had patronized its compilation

explanation: The Sangam literature historically known as "The poetry of the noble once" connects the ancient Tamil literature and is the earliest known literature of South India. The Tamil tradition and legends link it to three literary gatherings around Madurai and Kapatapuram. Most scholars suggest the historical Sangam literature era spanned from 300 BCE to 300 CE, while others variously place this early classical Tamil literature period a bit latter and more narrowly but all before 300 CE.

question: What are the two great epics that help to know the Sangam Age?

question: In the reign of which Chola King first trade delegation was sent to China?
explanation: Rajendra Chola I was one of the most successful monarchs of the South Indian Chola Empire. During his and Kulothunga Chola I's reign, commercial and political diplomats were sent to China. Cholas had good relations with the contemporary Chinese kingdoms.

question: Which of the following is not true about the Satavahanas?

options.a: They succeeded the Mauryas in the Deccan
options.b: Their rule coincided with the rule of the Andhras
options.c: They defeated the Kanvas and established their power in parts of Central India
options.d: They were able to conquer Delhi

explanation: The origin of the Satavahana dynasty is uncertain, but according to the puranas, their first king overthrew the Kanva dynasty. In the post-Maurya era, the Satavahanas established peace in the Deccan region and resisted the onslaught of foreign invaders. In particular their struggles with the Shaka western Satraps went on for a long time. The dynasty reached its Zenith under the rule of Gautamiputra Satakarni and his successor Vasisthiputra Pulamavi.

question: Which literature says the social political condition of Chola dynasty?
explanation: Jayamkondar was the poet literature of Kulottunga Chola. He is renowned for the poem Kalingattuparani. Kalingatthuparani literature says the social political condition of Chola dynasty.

question: The paintings in the Ajanta and Ellora caves are indicative of the development of art under the

Containing paintings & sculptures considered to be masterpieces of both Buddhist religious art & Universal pictorial Art.

question: Which of the following statements are correct? 1. The capital of Pallavas was at Kanchi 2. The emblem of the Pallavas was bull 3. The pallava king Mahendravarman had written a book on dance 4. The chola rulers were the followers of vaishnavism.

question: Which of the following is the architectural style of Somnath Temple?
explanation: Somnath temple is one of the twelve Aadi jyotirlingas of India, it is located at Veraval in Gujarat and it is believed to be the first among the Aadi Jyotirlingas in India. It was reconstructed many times in the past due to the repeated destruction by several invaders and rulers the present form of the Somnath Temple was reconstructed in Pre- chalukya style. Somnath temple, a highly revered shrine of Lord Shiva is held in very high esteem by the pious devotees all across the country and the globe.

The Cholas built a strong novel fleet to expand their empire. The Chola fleet represented the zenith of ancient Indian sea power.

question: Aryabhatt was a
explanation: Aryabhatt was the legendary Mathematician of the Gupta Era. He wrote 'Aryabhattiya' at the age of 23 years and later Arya-Siddhanta. He worked on the approximation for Pi, 62832/20000 = 3.1416. He also worked on the motions of the solar system and calculated the length of the solar year to 365.85, 868.05 days. Aryabhatta lived in Kusumapura in Pataliputra. Contribution of Aryabhatta Aryabhatta is the author of "Aryabhattiyam" which sketches his mathematical, planetary and cosmic theories. Aryabhatta deduced that earth is a rotating sphere. The stars do not move, it is the earth that rotates, its diameter is 1050 Yojanas. Its circumference is therefore 1050×13.6×π = 44,860 km.

question: Which of the Gupta rulers has been described as Napoleon of India?
explanation: Fa-Hien was a Chinese pilgrim who visited India during the reign of Chandragupta II on a religious mission. He lived in Pataliputra nearly three years, learned Sanskrit, and wrote the Vinaya rules.

question: What is the name of the king of Ceylon whom Samudragupta granted permission to build a monastic at Bodh Gaya?
explanation: The Gupta Emperor, Samudragupta granted permission to Buddhist King of Ceylon Meghavarman to build a monastery at Bodh Gaya. Samundragupta was a ruler of the Gupta Empire of Ancient India.

question: The University of Nalanda was set up by which Gupta ruler?
explanation: The University of Nalanda was established at Badagoan, Bihar in the 5th century during the reign of the Gupta Emperor Kumargupta I. During its peak time, it attracted several scholars and students even from foreign. Some scholars and students visited even from Tibet, China, Korea, and Central Asia.

question: During which rule of dynasty first self- immolation of widow took place in India?
explanation: During the reign of the Gupta dynasty self immolation for the first time finds mention in the Eran inscription of Bhanu Gupta of 510 AD in which there is a description of sati of the wife of the commander named Gopraj.

question: Which place of the ancient India was called ‘ ’ Awantika ?
explanation: Malwa place of ancient India was called 'Awantika' during 2nd century. It was held by the Mauryan and Gupta dynasty. This place is well- connected to the country's rail and road networks.

question: Who was the first Gupta ruler to attain the title of Maharajadhiraja?
explanation: Chandragupta I was a king of the Gupta Empire around 319 CE. Chandragupta I was son of Ghatotkacha and the grandson of Sri Gupta. Unlike his predecessor, whom we known as 'Mahraja', he came to be known as 'Maharajadhiraja'.

question: Who was the last ruler of the Gupta Dynasty?
explanation: The last ruler of the Gupta dynasty was Vishnu Gupta who ruled about till 550 CE. According to a Nalanda seal, Vishnu Gupta was son of Kumaragupta, and grandson of Purugupta. The empire was founded by the king Srigupta but the finest important ruler of the dynasty was Chandragupta I who established the prestige of dynasty and seems to have started the gupta era in CE 319–20 another important ruler was Samudragupta who was known as the Napoleon of India. question: Which one of the following was not responsible for the disintegration of the Gupta empire?

explanation: Too Much building activity during the early Gupta Period led to empty coffers was not the reason for disintegration of Gupta empire in this question. question: Chandra Gupta II was the first Gupta King to issue silver coins which were modeled on the coins of _______.
explanation: Chandragupta II, also known by title Vikramaditya, was the first Gupta king to issue silver coins. These coins were intended to replace the silver coinage of the Western Kshatrapas or Western Satrapas and were modeled on the Kshatrapa coinage.

question: The famous woman ruler of the Gupta period was:
explanation: Prabhavati was the daughter of Chandragupta II, the ruler of the Gupta dynasty. She was married in 410 AD to the Vakataka king Rudrasen II. After the death of her husband her son, Pravarsen II ruled under the Patronage of Prabhavati Gupta.

question: Chandragupt II got title of Vikramaditya meaning equal to :
explanation: A strong and vigorous ruler, Chandra Gupta II was well qualified to govern an extensive empire. Some of his silver coins having the title Vikramaditya ("Son of valour"), which suggests that he was the prototype for the king Vikramaditya of later tradition.

question: Which one of the following is associated with the Gupta Age of Indian history?
