explanation: The East India Company was formed by 31st some merchants of London on December 1600.

question: Who said 'The sword of revolution is sharpened on the whetting stone of ideas.'?

question: Who stated in his address to the Constituent Assembly that “We must hold fast to constitutional methods of achieving our social and economic objectives”?
explanation: Dr. B.R. Ambedkar while addressing Constituent Assembly said that "To maintain democracy we must hold fast to constitutional methods of achieving our social and economic objectives".

question: 'Direct Action' was the brain child of
explanation: ‘Direct Action’ was the brain child of Mohammad Ali Jinnah. In July, 1946 Jinnah held a press conference at his home in Bombay. He proclaimed that the Muslim league was " Preparing to launch a struggle" and that they "have chalked out a plan". He said that if the Muslims were not granted a separate Pakistan then they would launch "direct Action".

question: Before his unfortunate demise, for how many days Potti Sriramulu was on fast?
explanation: Potti Sriramulu was a Veteran Gandhian who went on a hunger strike for 58 days, demanding the formation of Andhra State to protect the interests of Telugu speakers. He died on 15 December 1952 after about of commencing his fast.

question: Who is known as "Kumaon Kesari"?
explanation: Badri Datta Pandey is popularly known as 'Kumaon Kesari' this title is derived after coolie – Begar movement in 1921.

question: Consider the following statements: (A) The raising of salt duty from 50 paise to one rupee in Surat in 1844, Caused a great discontent among the people. explanation: The raising of salt duty from 50 paise to one rupee in Surat in 1844, Caused a great discontent among the people. • Surat, one of the important city under the Bombay Presidency, had revolted against the salt tax in 1844 due to increment salt tax from 50 paisa to 1 Rupee. • Faced with a popular movement the government withdraw the additional salt levy.

question: Acharya Vinoba Bhave is known for :
explanation: Acharya Vinoba Bhave (1895–1982) was an advocate of non-violence activist, freedom activist, social reformer and spiritual teacher. Bhave observed the life of the average Indian living in a village and tried to find solution for the problem he faced with a firm spiritual foundation. This formed the core of his ‘Sarvodaya’ movement. He is best known for the Bhoodan movement.

question: Author of "Economic History of India" is :
explanation: Romesh Chunder Dutt is the author of "Economic History of India". It is one of the most widely read accounts of Indian economy under colonial rules. The Economic History of India documents and examines multilayered structural shifts in India's economy.

question: Who painted 'Tribute Money'?
explanation: The Tribute Money is one of many frescoes painted by Masaccio who is regard as the first great Italian painter of the Quattrocento period of Italian Renaissance. All of the frescos in the chapel tell the story of the life of St. Peter. The story of the tribute money is told in three separates scenes within the same fresco. This way of telling an entire story in one painting is called a continuous narrative. • Canaletto was an Italian painter. • Cimabue was an Italian painter and designer of mosaics from Florence. question: Who developed full roman typeface for the first time?
explanation: Nicholas Jenson (1420 – 1480) developed full Roman typeface for first time. He was a French engraver, pioneer, printer and type designer who carried out most of his work in Venice (Italy). Jenson acted as Master of the French Royal Mint at Tours, and is credited with being the 'creator of one of the finest early Roman typefaces'. Note : In Latin script typography, Roman is one of the three main kinds of historical type, alongside black letter and Italic. Roman type was modeled from a 15th European scribal manuscript style of the century, based on the pairing of inscriptional capitals used in ancient Rome with Carolingian minuscule developed in the holi Roman Empire.

question: S. Ramanujan is the first Indian to be elected as Fellow of the following College :

Ramanujan's discoveries have influenced several areas of Mathematics, but he is probably most famous for his contribution to number theory and infinite series, among them fascinating formulas that can be used to calculate (π) digits of Pi in unusual ways.

question: Brayer is also called :
explanation: A brayer is a hand-tool used historically in printing and printmaking to break up and "rub out" (spread) ink before it was "beaten" using inking balls or composition rollers. The word is derived from the verb to "bray", meaning to break, pound, or grind small, as in a mortar. A brayer consists of a short wooden cylinder with a handle fitted to one end, the other, flat end is used to rub the ink.

question: Carving having three sections hinged together is known as :
explanation: Triptych paintings first appeared in early Christian art beginning in the middle ages created as altarpieces, their primary function was to serve as an aid to devotion or prayer. Typically, the central composition shows the main narrative of the story, while the left and right panels include additional details and sometimes, depict the patrons or commissioners of the painting. The three panel format of a triptych had several interesting meanings and functions. The hinges made it easier to store and transport the painting since it would be smaller and more compact when folded, while also protecting the painted surface.

question: Under whom leadership the interim government was formed?

question: The idea of Ombudsman is taken from which of the following countries?
explanation: The idea of Ombudsman is taken from Sweden. Use of this term began in Sweden, with the Swedish Parliamentary Ombudsman instituted by the Instrument of Government of 1809, to safeguard the rights of citizens by establishing a supervisory agency independent of the executive branch.

question: Which Act legally recognized the principle of election to the legislative councils?

question: On which date the objectives Resolution was adopted by the Constituent Assembly?
explanation: The Resolution was unanimously adopted by the Constituent Assembly on 22nd of January, 1947. On 29th August 1947, the Constituent Assembly set up a Drafting Committee under the chairmanship of Dr. B.R. Ambedkar to prepare Draft Constitution of India.

question: Which of the following statements about Montague-Chelmsford Reforms is/are correct? A. It made the central legislative bicameral. B. The Act explicitly mentioned the appointment of three Indian members to the Council. Select the code for the correct answer from the options given below:
explanation: The Central Legislative Council was replaced by a bicameral consisting of an Upper House and a Lower House. The majority of members were elected by direct election. Note– Montague Chelmsford reforms (1919) were introduced by the colonial government to introduce self- governing institutions gradually in British India.

question: The Presidential Address is prepared by

question: If a group of rich people use power for their selfish goals, it is called as–
explanation: If a group of rich people use power for their selfish goals, it is called as Oligarchy. Oligarchy is a form of power structure in which power rests with rich or wealthy people for their selfish purposes.

question: Which country has the lengthiest Constitution?
explanation: The Constitution of India is the longest (the lengthiest) written Constitution of any country in the world, originally having 395 Articles, 22 Parts and 8 Schedules. question: Who propounded the idea of 'Total Revolution' in Indian Politics?

question: The Constitution of India was adopted by the Constituent Assembly on :
explanation: The Constitution of India was adopted by the Constituent Assembly on 26th November 1949 and became effective on 26 January 1950. question: The first session of Constituent Assembly of India was held in which of the following cities?
explanation: The first meeting of the Constituent 9th Assembly was held on December 1946 in New Delhi at Constitutional Hall (now the central house of parliament). In the first session Dr. Sachchidananda Sinha was elected temporary President of the Assembly, in accordance with French practice. • The last session of the constituent assembly was held on 24th January 1950 and at the same day the assembly elected its President Dr. Rajendra Prasad as the first President of free India.

question: Which one of the following derives its power most from the system of delegated legislation?
explanation: Delegated legislation also referred to as secondary legislation, is legislation made by a person or body other than Parliament. It is a process by which the executive authority is given power by primary legislation to make law in order to implement and administer the requirement of that primary legislation. The legislative committees derived its power most from the system of delegated legislation.

question: At present time how many Zonal Councils are working in India?

question: In which of the following countries the system of judicial review was originated?
explanation: Judicial review system was originated in the USA. In Indian Constitution it is the power of the Supreme Court to examine the Constitutionality of legislative enactments and executive orders of both the Central and State governments and can declare them illegal if found volatile of the Constitution. Judicial review is needed to uphold the Supremacy of the Constitution, to maintain federal equilibrium and to protect Fundamental Rights. The difference between USA and Indian judicial review system is that in USA the Constitution provides for 'due process of law' while Indian Constitution contained the 'procedure established by Law.

'Economic justice' means justice from the stand point of economic force. In short, it means equal pay for equal work. Economic Justice aims to create opportunities for every person to have a dignified, productive and creative life. 'Social Justice' means the abolition of all sorts of inequalities which may result from the inequalities of wealth, status, race, caste and the like.

As per the 52nd Amendment Act 1985 a 'defection' by one third of the elected members of a political party was considered as a 'merger'. explanation: By Article 3 of the Indian Constitution, Parliament has the power by law to form a new state from the territory of any state or by uniting two or more states, increase or decrease the area of any state, alter the boundaries or the name of any state. The conditions laid down for the making of such a law are - (i) Such a bill must be introduced only on the recommendation of the President. A simple majority and ordinary legislature procedure are enough for Parliament to form new states or alter existing state boundaries.

question: Which one of the following rights, is not a Fundamental Right in Indian Constitution?

In 1978 with the 44th Amendment to the Constitution, which deleted the Fundamental Right to property from the Constitution. • Article 300A was inserted in the Constitution, which said that 'no person would be deprived of their property save by authority of law'. It's now only a legal right.

question: Which Part of the Constitution is described as the ‘Magna Carta’ of India?
explanation: Part III of the Constitution is described as the Magna Carta of India. • “Magna Carta” or The Great Charter of Right issued by King John of England in 1215 was the first written document relating to the Fundamental Rights of citizens. • Magna Carta is significant because it is a statement of low that applied to the kings as well as the his subjects.

question: Who is the custodian of the Fundamental Rights of Indian Citizens?

question: The "Right of Constitutional Remedies" is mentioned in
explanation: Article 32 deals with the "Right to Constitutional Remedies" or affirms the right to move the Supreme Court by appropriate proceedings for the enforcement of the rights conferred in Part-III of the Constitution. question: Under this, the State shall not deny to any person equality before law with the territory of India. This right cannot be denied to every person whether he is a citizen or a foreigner. explanation: Article 21 of the Indian Constitution states that no person shall be deprived of his life or personal liberty except according to the procedure established by law. The procedure established by law has been taken from the Constitution of Japan. The due process doctrine is followed in the United States of America, and Indian Constitutional framers purposefully left that out. But in most of the recent judgments of the Supreme Court, the due process aspect is coming into the picture again. 209 / 896

question: Which of the following Articles in the Constitution is considered as heart and soul of the Constitution as per Dr. B. R. Ambedkar?
explanation: Article 32 of the Indian Constitution is considered as the heart and soul of the Constitution as per Dr. B.R. Ambedkar. It allows an individual to approach the Supreme Court if she or he believes that her or his Fundamental Rights have been violated or they need to be enforced.

question: The Parliament of India enacted the Untouchability (Offences) Act, 1955, in exercise of the power conferred under
explanation: The Parliament of India enacted the untouchability offences Act 1955 in exercise of the power conferred under Article 17 of the Indian Constitution.

question: Which Article of the Constitution of India prohibits employment of children in factories?
explanation: Article 24 Prohibition of employment of children in factories, etc. No child below the age of fourteen years shall be employed to work in any factory or mine or engaged in any other hazardous employment.

question: Under Article 32 of the Constitution of India, the Supreme Court is invested with the power to issue writs. Which of the following is NOT one of those writs?
explanation: A writ petition can be termed as a formal written order issued by a judicial authority who possesses the authority to do so. The meaning of the word 'Writs' means command in writing in the name of the Court. It is a legal document issued by the Court that orders a person or entity to perform a specific act or to cease performing a specific action or deed. There are five types of writs as provided under Article 32 of the Constitution

question: Mandamus 4. Certiorari 5. Prohibition Note-The High Court has the jurisdiction under Article 226 to issue directions, orders or writs for the enforcement of Fundamental Rights. 232. Which one of the following Articles of the Indian Constitution is related to 'Right to Education' ?
explanation: The Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act or Right to Education Act (RTE), is an act of the Parliament of India enacted on 4 August 2009 which describes the modalities of the importance of free and compulsory education for children between 6 and 14 in India under Article 21A of the India Constitution. India became one of 135 countries to make education a Fundamental Right of every child when the act came into force on 1 April 2010.

question: Which of the following Articles of the Constitution of India deals with the abolition of titles?

options.a: Article 5 – Citizenship at the commencement of the
options.b: Article 26 – Freedom to manage religious affairs.

explanation: Article 18 of the Constitution of India deals with the abolition of titles.

question: Which of the following Articles the Constitution of India deals with the protection of interests of minorities?
explanation: Article 29 and Article 30 guarantee certain right to the minorities. Article 29 protects the interests of the minorities by making a provision that any citizen/section of citizens having a distinct language, script or culture have the right to conserve the same.

question: Fundamental Rights are called as–––.
explanation: The Fundamental Rights were included in the CONSTITUTION because they were considered essential for the development of the personality of every individual and to preserve human dignity. • There are six Fundamental Rights recognized by the Indian Constitution. • Fundamental Rights are justiciable and allow persons to move to the courts for their enforcement, if and when they are violated.

question: Which one of the following is a Human Right as well as a Fundamental Right under the Constitution of India?
explanation: Article 21 of Constitution of India– Protection of Life and Personal Liberty. • Article 21 states that “No person shall be deprived of his life or personal liberty except according to a procedure established by law.” • Article 21 of the Constitution of India, provides for right to life and also right to live with human dignity. The right to clean environment and further, pollution free water has been protected under the broad rubric of the right to life guaranteed under Article 21. Article 20 : No person shall be convicted of any offence except for violation of the law in force. Article 22 : Protection against arrest and detention in certain cases. Article 23 : Prohibition of traffic in human beings and forced labours.

question: Which of the following Articles of the Constitution of India provides the right to assemble peaceably and without arms?
explanation: Article 19(1) (b) guarantees to all citizens of India "rights to Assemble peaceably and without arms". • This right includes the rights to hold meetings and to take out processions. 211 / 896 • The right is subject to the following restrictions- (i) The assembly must be peaceful and harmonious. (ii) It must be unarmed and not threatening the safety of the people. (iii) Reasonable restrictions can be imposed under clause 3 of Article 19.

question: Which of the following is NOT listed as a Fundamental Right in the Indian Constitution?

options.a: Equality of opportunities in matters of public employment

explanation: Article 39A of the Constitution of India provides for free legal aid to the poor and weaker sections of the society to promote justice on the basis of equal opportunity. The Fundamental Rights are defined in Part 3 of Indian Constitution from Article 12 to 35.

question: Which of the following is NOT included in the Directive Principles of State Policy of the Constitution of India?

options.d: Equal justice and free legal aid

explanation: Right to Information is an act of the Parliament of India which sets out the rules and procedures regarding citizen's Right to Information. Right to Information Act 2005 mandates timely response to citizen requests for government information. It is not included in the Directive Principles of State Policy. It is legal right provided by the act of Parliament. Articles 36-51 under Part-IV of Indian Constitution deal with Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSP). They are borrowed from the Constitution of Ireland which had copied it from the Spanish Constitution. Indian Constitution has not been originally classified DPSPs but on the basis of their contnet and direction, they are usually classified into three types-Socialistic Principles, Gandhian Principles and Liberal Intellectual Principles. Equal pay for equal work - Article 39. Equal justice and free legal aid - Article 39A Organisation of village Panchayats - Article 40.

question: Who among the following is the guardian of the Indian Constitution and the protector of Fundamental Rights of the citizens?
explanation: Article 13 of the Indian Constitution declares that any law, which infringes on the Fundamental Rights, shall be void. According to Article 32 of the Constitution of India. The Supreme Court and the High Court under Article 226 have been empowered to issue writs for the protection of Fundamental Rights, that is why the judiciary has been called the protector of the Indian Constitution and the protector of Fundamental Rights.

question: Which of the following provision can be amended by the special majority of Parliament?
explanation: • Amendment in Fundamental Rights needs a Special majority in Parliament. • Amendment in Fundamental Rights can only be given effect by bringing in a Constitutional Amendment Bill.

question: Which of the following is Human Right under Universal Declaration of Human Rights?

options.c: Right to equal access to public services

explanation: The Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR) is a historic document that was adopted by the United Nations General Assembly on 10 December 1948 as Resolution 217. This includes Political and Civil Right such as the Right to Education, The Right to Food, The Right to Equal Access to Public Services, the Right to Freedom and more.

question: According to the Constitution of India which of the following is enforceable in the court?
explanation: The Fundamental Rights are enforceable in the High Court and Supreme Court. Fundamental Rights are the basic and inalienable rights of all citizens contained in Part III (Article 12 to 35) of Constitution of India. These rights universally apply to all citizens, irrespective of race, place of birth, caste or gender. The six Fundamental Rights recognized by the Constitution are the Right to Equality, Right to Freedom, Right against Exploitation, Right to Freedom of Religion, Culture and Educational Rights, Right to Constitutional Remedies.

question: Which one of the following does not come under social rights?
explanation: Fundamental Rights in India are the rights guaranteed under part III (Article 12-35) of the Constitution of India. There are six Fundamental Rights (Article 12-35) recognized by the Indian Constitution. (i) Right to equality (Article 14-18) (ii) Right to freedom (Article 19-22) (iii) Right to against exploitation (Article 23-24) (iv) Right to freedom of religion (Article 25-28) (v) Cultural and educational rights (Article 29-30) (vi) Right to Constitutional remedies While the Constitution also provides some other rights, such as the right to vote, that are not Fundamental (social) Rights. 212 / 896

question: Some of the Fundamental Rights are not available to the members of the armed forces. Who reserves the right to decide the same?

options.b: Right to Freedom of Speech and Expression
options.c: Right to Protection of Life and Personal

explanation: During National Emergency, Article 20 (protection in respect of conviction for offences) & Article 21 (protection of life and personal liberty) cannot be suspended. Article 359, of the Constitution authorizes the President of India to suspend the right to move any court for enforcement of Fundamental Rights during National Emergency except Article 20 and Article 21.

question: Articles 23 & 24 of the Indian Constitution deal with
explanation: Article 23 and 24 of Indian Constitution is deals with Right against exploitation. Article 23 States the Prohibition of human trafficking and forced labour, whereas Article 24 States the Prohibition of employment of children below the age of 14 years in factories, mines, shops, etc.

question: The Fundamental Rights of Indian citizen are contained in :

It gives Right to equality, including equality before law, prohibition of discrimination on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex or place of birth, and equality of opportunity in matters of employment. • Part VIII of Constitution is related to the Union Territories. Polity • Part IV of Constitution is related to DPSP (Directive Principles of State Policy). • The Seventh Schedule of the Constitution defines and specifies allocation of powers and functions between Union and States.

question: Assertion (A): Reservation has lost its Constitutional purpose. Reason (R): There is plea for and grant of reservation to all sections of people. Find the correct answer from the under noted code. Code:

question: In which year the Freedom of Information Act was replaced by the Right to Information Act?
explanation: The Right to Information is a Fundamental Right under Article 19(1) of the Indian Constitution. The RTI Bill was passed by Parliament of India on 15 June 2005 and came into force with effect from 12 October 2005. It replaced the former Freedom of Information Act, 2002.

question: Consider the following statements in relation to the rights of a child in India : 1. It prohibits employment of children below the age of 16 years in factories. 2. explanation: Right to education has been given to all Indian children between the ages of 6 to 14 years under 86th the Constitutional Amendment Act 2002 Indian governments at the centre since 1993 working towards bringing a Constitutional Amendment to make education a Fundamental Right in the Indian Constitution. This led to the 86th Amendment in December 2002 which inserted Article 21A in the Indian Constitution. Article 21A states that the State shall provide free and compulsory education to all children of the age of six to fourteen years.

The women once held high social status and were well respected but now they are nothing more than sex slaves. The practice of Devadasi system is the violation of Constitutional principles like Article 23 (prohibition of traffic in human beings and forced labour), right to life (Article 21) etc.

question: Under which Right in our Constitution is 'Child Labour' prohibited?

explanation: Article 51A of the Indian Constitution deals with the Fundamental Duties. India has borrowed the concept of Fundamental Duties from the USSR (The Soviet Union). explanation: The Union Government gives grant-in-aid to the States on the recommendations of the Finance Commission. • A grant-in-aid is money coming from Central Government for a specific project. • Such funding is usually used when the Government and the legislature decide that the recipient should be publicly funded but operate with reasonable independence from the State • Grant- in- aid is comes under the Article 275 that is Grants from the Union to certain States.

question: India is a Welfare State because of the provisions of the......
explanation: The Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSP) enshrined in Part IV of the Indian Constitution reflects that India is a Welfare State. • India is called a Welfare State because it is founded on the concept of equal opportunities. Seat are reserved for Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes in government jobs, educational institutions, Lok Sabha and Vidhan Sabha.

question: Parliament can amend the Constitution With–– –majority.
explanation: The Constitution of India does not provide an explicit classification for the kind of majorities needed for passing various kinds of Bills, a careful interpretation of the Constitution provides four major types of majorities – (1) Simple majority – More than 50% of members present and voting. (2) Absolute majority – More than 50% of total strength of the House. (3) Special majority – Generally 2/3rd of total strength of the House or 2/3rd of present and voting. (4) Effective majority – More than 50% of strength of the House (strength is calculated by subtracting vacant seats from total strength).

question: How long can one, under the Constitution, remain minister even without being a member of any of the two Houses of the Parliament?
explanation: Under the Article 75(5) person who is not Member of Parliament (MP) can be appointed as a minister. But, he/she should be able to become an MP within 6 months. •He can either be a member of Lok Sabha or Rajya Sabha. Polity

question: As a non-member, who can participate in the proceedings of either House of the Parliament?
explanation: "Attorney General" can 'participate in the proceedings' of either "House of Parliament". The "President of India" appoints the Attorney General under the "Article 76" of the Indian Constitution.

question: Which of the following is an essential feature of Parliamentary Government?
explanation: The Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the House of people (Lok Sabha). According to Article 74 there will be Council of Ministers. The Cabinet's role is to advise the President on any subject. The head of the State holds a ceremonial position and is the nominal executive. In India the head of government is the Prime Minister who is the real executive and executive is a part of legislature which is an essential feature of Parliamentary Government. Separation of Power– It signifies the division of different powers in between various organs of the State, Executive, Legislature and Judiciary. Proportional Representation– It characterizes electoral systems in which divisions in an electorate are reflected proportionately in the elected body.

question: Assertion (A): There is a President, but no Presidency in India. Reason (R): There is a Council of Ministers. Find the correct answer from the code given below: Code:

options.a: Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A).
options.b: Both (A) and (R) are true, but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A).

explanation: India has adopted the Parliamentary system from Britain in which the head of State is only a nominal head, therefore it is said that there is a President in India but there is no Presidential system. Both India and Britain have a Council of Ministers made up of some members including the Prime Minister and the Cabinet, which undertakes the work of law making. Hence, both (A) & (R) are true but (R) is not correct explanation of (A).

question: What is correct about the joint Session of Parliament? (A) To summon for the joint session a discretionary power of president (B) On the recommendation of Parliament President summons for joint session. (C) It is presided over by the Speaker. (D) In the absence of Speaker, it is presided over by the Deputy Chairman of Rajya Sabha. 218 / 896 (E) It considers on the matter of deadlock. (F) It considers on Finance Bill too.
explanation: Article 108 of the Indian constitution is related to joint sitting of both the Houses of Parliament. The joint sitting of the Parliament is called by the President and is presided by the Speaker or, in his absence, by the Deputy Speaker of Lok Sabha or in his absence by the Deputy Chairman of Rajya Sabha.

question: In the Parliamentary form of government the Executive is :
explanation: In the Parliamentary system the Chief Executive of the State (Prime Minister) is not elected directly by the people, but he/she is normally the leader of the majority party in the Parliament, he chooses his own Cabinet. In a Parliamentary system, powers are centered in the Parliament, the legislature takes the responsibility of government.

question: On which matter Rajya Sabha is equal or powerful than Lok Sabha ? (A) To create a new state (B) To create a new All India Service (C) To declare a subject of State list of National importance (D) To remove the Dy. Chairman of Rajya Sabha (E) To initiate the removal motion. (F) To initiate the removal motion against President (G) On Constitutional Amendment
explanation: Rajya Sabha enjoys equal powers with Lok Sabha in matters like impeachment of President, removal of Vice President, Constitutional Amendments and removal of judges of Supreme Court & High Court. Rajya Sabha is a permanent House. It can authorize the Parliament to make a law on a subject enumerated in the State list (Article 249). It can authorize the Parliament to create new all India services common to both the Centre & States (Article 312).

question: Which of the following is the Indian contribution to Parliamentary procedures?
explanation: Zero Hour/Zero Session– The time immediately following the Question Hour has come to be know as “Zero Hour”. It begin around 12 noon and member can, with prior notice to the Speaker raise issues of important during this time. Primary objective of an adjournment motion is to draw the attention of Lok Sabha to a recent matter of urgent public importance with regard to which a motion or a resolution with proper notice will be too late. Zero Session/Hour is the Indian contribution to Parliamentry procedures. Polity

question: Punchhi Commission is related to:

question: If there is a sharing of power between the Union and the State Government, it is called :
explanation: Federalism is a system of Government in which the same territory is Governed by Union & the State Government. In this forms of Government powers are divided between Union & State Government. • Fascism is a nationalistic, top-down system with rigid class roles that is ruled by all powerful dictator. • Communism is a system based around a theory of economic equality and advocates for a classless society. • Oligarchy government by the few, especially despotic power excercised by a small and privileged group of corrupt or selfish purpose.
chapter: Centre State and Inter State Relation

question: Consider statement and Reason, choose the correct answer from the codes below : Assertions (A) : Federal state is guided by strong desire for national unity. Reason (R) : It is developed out of necessity for union of independent states

options.a: Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A).
options.b: Both (A) and (R) are true (R) is not correct explanation of (A).

explanation: In federal systems, political authority is divided between two autonomous sets of government, one national and other sub-national, both of which operate directly upon the people.

question: The cut-off date for updating National Register of Citizens in Assam is
explanation: National Register of Citizens (NRC) was first introduced after the 1951 census of India and Assam was the first in updating this NRC for including the names of the persons along with their descendants whose name was successfully registered in the NRC of 1951, or was found in any of the electoral rolls till the 24th midnight of March 1971. Polity (cid:1) The main purpose for the introduction and updating of the NRC in Assam was the identification of the illegal immigrants in Assam from Bangladesh during the 1971 war with Pakistan.

question: How many subjects are mentioned in Union List of India?
explanation: The Union List of India contains 97 (currently 100) subjects, on which only the Union has the right to make laws. There were a total of 66 subjects in the State list (currently 61) and a total of 47 subjects in the Concurrent list (currently 52).
chapter: Centre State and Inter State Relation

question: Through which Article, the Parliament can enact a law even on the State subjects, in the interest of Nation?
explanation: Through Article 249, Parliament has the right to legislate on subjects mentioned in the State List in the national interest. A total of 66 (currently 61) subjects are currently mentioned in the State list. • The Union list is a list of 100 subjects (originally 97) and Concurrent list has list of 47 subjects (currently 52) as provided in the Seventh Schedule to the Indian Constitution.
chapter: Centre State and Inter State Relation

question: Who has the power to set up Inter-State Council?
explanation: The Constitution of India in Article 263, provided that an Inter State Council (ISC) maybe established “if at any time it appears to the President that the public interests would be served by the establishment of a Council.” • It was established at 28 May 1990 on the recommendation of Sarkaria Commission.
chapter: Centre State and Inter State Relation

question: The subject on which both the Centre and State Governments can legislate is contained in :
explanation: The subject on which both the Centre and State Governments can legislate are contained in ‘the Concurrent list’. Residuary list:- It is the list which contains subjects that do not comes under Union List, State list, or Concurrent list. These Subjects came into use after the Indian Constitution was made. These subjects are e- commerce, computer software and etc. This list falls within the jurisdiction of Union Government. Union List:- The subjects under the Union List are Defense, Army, International relations, Ports, Railways, Highways, Communication etc. State list:- The subject are- Public order, Police, Public Health and Sanitation, Hospitals and dispensaries, Betting and Gambling. Note– The Constitution divided the powers between the Centre and the States in terms of Union List, State List and Concurrent List in the Seventh Schedule. 220 / 896
chapter: Centre State and Inter State Relation

question: What was the primary objective of Sarkaria Commission?
explanation: The main objective of the Sarkaria Commission was to make recommendations on the balance of Power Related to Center-State relations. Sarkaria Commission was Constituted by the Government of India in June 1983 under the Chairmanship of Justice Ranjit Singh Sarkaria, retired Judge of the Supreme Court.
chapter: Centre State and Inter State Relation

question: Under which Article of the Constitution of India are taxes levied and collected by the Centre and assigned to the States?
explanation: The 7th Schedule of the Indian Constitution deals with the division of powers between the Union Government and State Government. The division of powers between Union and State is notified through three kinds of the list mentioned.

explanation: Sarkaria Commission was setup in 1983 by the Central government of India. The Sarkaria Commission's charter was to examine the Centre State relationship. Sarkaria Commission report contained 247 specific recommendations.

explanation: The legislative section of Constitution of India is divided into three lists : Union list, State list and Concurrent list. Concurrent list is a list of 52 7th items. It is given in the Schedule to the Constitution of India. Concurrent list–

question: Who was the Chairman of the Godavari Water Dispute Tribunal?
explanation: R.S Bachawat was the Chairman of the Godavari water dispute tribunal. Government of India Constituted a common water utilization disputes about the River basin States of Godavari and Krishna Rivers under the provisions of interstate River water disputes Act 1956. The common tribunal was headed by Sri R.S. Bachawat as its Chairman with Sri D. M. Bhandari and Sri D.M. Sen as its members.

question: The disputes between Government of India and one more states can be heard by Supreme Court under its
explanation: Original jurisdiction means that the Supreme Court has the authority to here and determine in the first instances the cases which cannot be moved in any other Court than the Supreme Court. The Supreme Court entertains original jurisdiction in the following cases of dispute between Government of India and one or more States, or any States, or all States under Article (131). Advisory Jurisdiction – The Supreme Court has advisory jurisdiction over matters that have been specifically referred to it by the President of India under Article 143 of the Constitution. Appellate Jurisdiction – The Supreme Court has also a very wide appellate jurisdiction over all Courts and tribunals in India in as much as it may, in its discretion, grant special leave to appeal under Article 136 of the Constitution from any judgment, decree, determination sentence or order in any cause or matter passed or made by any Court. 221 / 896

question: The President can proclaim Emergency if he/she finds emergency condition and security threat for India under Article:
explanation: Part Eighteen of the Indian Constitution deals with the Emergency provisions in Articles 352 to 360. There are three type of Emergencies mentioned in the Constitution. The power of imposing all three types of Emergencies is vested upon the President of India. (1) Article 352- National Emergency (i.e.-war, external aggression or armed rebellion) (2) Article 356- President’s Rule (i.e.- Failure of the Constitutional machinery of the state) (3) Article 360- Financial Emergency (i.e.- When a state arises in the country which leads to a financial crisis in India.)

question: Which of the following in not correct about the borrowed Features of Indian Constitution?

options.a: The concept of Concurrent list is borrowed from Australia
options.b: The Fundamental duties are borrowed from Russia
options.c: The Amendment of the Constitution is borrowed from France
options.d: The method of Election of the President is borrowed from Ireland

explanation: The Procedure for Amendment in the Indian Constitution is borrowed from South Africa.

question: State Emergency can be extended beyond one year if following conditions are fulfilled. In this regard which is the correct condition?

options.a: Emergency under Article 352 in force in either whole of India or in the state.
options.b: Election Commission certifies that there are difficulties in holding elections in that state

explanation: Article 352 says that if the President is satisfied that a grave Emergency exists where by the security of India or any part of the territory of India is threatened by was or external aggression or armed rebellion, he may proclaim an Emergency. This Emergency many be with respect to the whole or part of India.

question: What is the duration of the President’s Rule in a State in the first instance?
explanation: President Rule (Article 356):– •Proclamation of President's Rule must be approved by both the Houses of Parliament within two months from the date of its issue, and this approval takes Place through a simple majority. •The President Rule is valid for six months and it can be extended for a maximum period of 3 years with the approval of the Parliament, every six months. •It is also known as “Constitutional Emergency”, In case of failure of Constitutional Machinery, the state Legislature is suspended and the executive authority of the state is assumed by the President in whole or in a part. Polity

question: Article ______ empowers the President to declare financial emergency
explanation: Financial Emergency Article 360 – • President can proclaim a Financial Emergency if financial stability or credit of India or any part of the territory is threatened. • This proclamation must be approved by the Parliament within two month. • This type of Emergency has not been declared so far. Article 368 :- Power of Parliament to amend the Constitution and procedure Article 356 :- Under Article 356, if a State Government is unable to function according to Constitutional provisions, the Union Government can take direct control of the State. Article 365 :- Article 365 is an extension to Emergency powers of President.

question: Article –––– of the Constitution of India relates to provisions for proclaiming emergency by the President of India.
explanation: The President can declare three types of Emergencies National, State and Financial Emergency. • National Emergency under Article 352. • State Emergency is under Article 356 • Financial Emergency comes under Article 360 Note- Article 72 says that the President shall have the power to grant pardons, reprieves, respites or remissions of punishment or suspend, remit or commute the sentence of any person convicted of any offence.

question: Which one of the following cannot be the ground for proclamation of Emergency under the Constitution of India?
explanation: The Emergency provisions are contained in Part-18 of the Indian Constitution from Articles 352 to

question: The Constitution stipulates three types of Emergencies– (i) National Emergency due to war, External Aggression or Armed Rebellion (Article 352) (ii) President's Rule due to the failure of the Constitutional Machinery in the State. (Article 356) (iii) Financial Emergency due to a threat to the financial stability or credit of India (Article 360). • Emergency can't be proclaimed on the basis of Internal disturbances. 327. A Proclamation of Emergency must be placed before the Parliament for its approval within
explanation: The National Emergency proclamation can be withdrawn at any time by the President. If the proclamation is not withdrawn by the President within One Month, it should be approved by the both Houses of Parliament within one month. Otherwise, it will not remain enforce after one month. If the proclamation is made when the Lok Sabha has been dissolved or the dissolution of the Lok Sabha takes place within one month without the approval of the Emergency Proclamation, the Emergency Proclamation shall be enforce after the expiry of 30 days from the date of the first sitting of the Lok Sabha. Will not be there the Emergency Proclamation must be approved by a 2/3rd (two-thirds) majority of the Parliament. Once approved, the Emergency Proclamation will remain enforce for six months and re-approval by Parliament is required to continue. Note : If the Lok Sabha passes a resolution to withdraw the Emergency Proclamation by a simple majority, the President will have to withdraw the Proclamation.

question: Under which of the following Articles of the Indian Constitution, President's rule can be imposed in the States?
explanation: Under Article 356 of the Indian Constitution, President's rule can be imposed on the failure of the Constitutional Machinery in the States. President's rule under Article 356 was first implemented in the State of Punjab and Patiala and East Punjab State Union in 1951.

question: National Emergency can be proclaimed on the ground(s) of 1. External aggression 2. Internal disturbance 3. War 4. Armed rebellion Select the correct answer from the codes given below :
explanation: After the 40th Amendment Act 1978, National Emergency can only be declared on ground's due to "External aggression or War", also called as External-Emergency and on the ground of "Armed Rebellion", also called as Internal Emergency.

question: By the Indian Constitution, who has the power to make a 'Proclamation of Emergency'?
explanation: According to Article 358, when a proclaimation of National Emergency under Article 352 is made, the six Fundamental Rights under Article 19 are automatically suspended. Note :– Article 352 is proclaimed on the grounds of War or External aggression.

question: Consider the following statements in respect of provisions as to Financial Emergency: I. A proclamation of Financial Emergency has to be laid before each House of Parliament. II. A proclamation of Financial Emergency ceases to operate at the expiry of six months, unless before the expiry of that period it has been approved by resolutions of both Houses of Parliament. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Article 360 empowers the President to proclaim a Financial Emergency if he/she is satisfied that a situation has arisen due to which the financial stability or credit of India or any part of its Territory is threatened. A proclamation declaring Financial Emergency must be approved by both the Houses of Parliament within two months from the date of its issue.

question: Financial Emergency may be declared by the President in which of the following case?

options.a: If he is satisfied such a situation has arisen
options.b: On suggestion made by the Prime Minister

explanation: Article 360 of Indian Constitution provides power to the President to proclaim a Financial Emergency if he/she is satisfied that a situation has arisen due to which the financial stability or credit of India or any part of its territory is threatened. A proclamation declaring Financial Emergency must be approved by both the Houses of Parliament within two months from the date of its issue.

question: Which of the following are correct regarding the effects of National Emergency?

options.d: Effect on Right to Life and Personal Liberty 223 / 896 as right.

explanation: or (b) or (c) : A proclamation of Emergency has drastic and wide ranging effects on the political system. These consequences can be grouped into three categories. (i) Effect on the Centre-State relations. (ii) Effect on the life of the Lok Sabha and State assembly. (iii) Effect on the Fundamental Rights. • Article 359 deals with suspension of Fundamental Rights except Article 20 and 21 (Right to Life and Personal Liberty).

question: Parliament Structures and It's Function 335. Parliament or State Legislature can declare a seat vacant; if any member of the house remains absent for a period of ________without seeking primary consent.
explanation: Article 74(1) of the Constitution provides that-there shall be a Council of Ministers with the Prime Minister as its head to aid and advise the President. • Article 79 of the Constitution provides for a Parliament consisting of an Elected President and the two Houses, the House of people (Lok Sabha) and the Council of States (Rajya Sabha). • Andhra Pradesh, Karnataka, Maharashtra, Telangana, Uttar Pradesh and Bihar have bicameral Legislatures. • Presidential form of Government is a feature of American Government.

question: The Parliament is not entitled to amend the basic structure of the Constitution. This was the Judgment by Supreme Court in.

options.c: Kesavananda Bharati V. State of Kerala
options.d: Shankari Prasad V. Union of India Polity

explanation: Kesavananda Bharati V. State of Kerala case is a landmark judgment of Supreme Court of India that outlined the basic structure doctrine of the Indian Constitution. • The Doctrine forms the basis of power of the Indian judiciary to review and override Amendments to the Constitution of India enacted by the Indian Parliament.

question: 'Collective responsibility' is one of the important features of ----- system.
explanation: Collective responsibility means that all Ministers are bound by the collective decisions made by Cabinet. Parliamentary system is also known as Cabinet form of government in which Prime Minister is responsible for appointing the salient Ministers.

question: The Speaker can ask a member of the House to stop speaking and let another member speak. This phenomenon is known as:
explanation: The Speaker of Lok Sabha can ask a member of the House to stop speaking and let another member speak, it is known as yielding the floor. • Decorum – Maintaining proper behaviour in the House. • Interpellation – A Parliamentary procedure of demanding that a government official explains some act or policy. • Crossing the Floor – ‘Crossing the Floor’ occurs when a member of a Parliamentary party walks across the chamber to vote against their party.

question: Which of the following is unmatched in the options in view of election? i. President ii. Vice President iii. Rajya Sabha iv. Lok Sabha v. State Legislative Assembly vi. State Legislative Council Options :
explanation: Members of the Lok Sabha (lower House) and State Legislative Assembly are directly elected by the people of India to serve a 5 year term by single member constituencies. (State Legislative Assembly – Article 168) (Lower House/Lok Sabha – Article 79) • President, Vice President (Article 52, Article 63 respectively) are elected by Indirect election system. • Members of Rajya Sabha and State Legislative Council are elected and nominated by indirect election system and by President and governor respectively. (Vidhan Parishad/State Legislative Council-Article 169)

question: –––– is the highest law-making body in India.
explanation: Parliament is the supreme legislative body of India comprised of the President and the two Houses Raj Sabha & Lok Sabha. Therefore Parliament is the highest law- making body is India.

question: According to Indian Constitution, who decides the Salary of members of Parliament?
explanation: According to Article 106 of Indian Constitution the Parliament will decide the salary and allowances, of every Members of Parliament. It is important that to be elected as a Member of Parliament one must be a citizen of India. For the membership of Rajya Sabha, age must not be less than 30 years and 25 years in the case of Lok Sabha.

question: A starred question is asked by an MP and answered orally by the:

options.e: Any MP from the treasury benches

explanation: A starred question is one to which a member desires an oral answer in the House and which is distinguished by an asterisk mark, when a question is answered orally, supplementary Questions can beasked thrown. Only 20 qauestion can be listed for oral answer on a day. The answers are given by Ministers in charge.

question: The primary function of ––––– is to as an important link between two House of Parliament and the Government to efficiently handle the diverse parliamentary work on behalf of the Government of India in the Parliament.
explanation: The task of efficiently handling diverse and enormous Parliamentary work on be half of the Government in the Parliament has been assigned to the Ministry of Parliamentary Affairs.

question: Who is the Head of the Government in India?
explanation: Head of the Government is the Prime Minister. He is the real head as President is the nominal head. He is the ducted representative of the people. The Prime Minister is the senior most of Cabinet in the executive of government in a Parliamentary system. Although the Prime Minister and Cabinet are not directly mentioned in the Constitution. Polity

question: There are three organs in a government. Choose the wrong answer :
explanation: The three branches of the government are the Executive, the Legislature and the Judiciary. In India the separation of functions rather than of power is followed. Unlike in the USA, in India the concept of separation of the powers is not adhered to strictly. However a system of checks and balances have been put in place in such a manner that the Judiciary has the power to strike down any unConstitutional laws passed by the legislature.

question: Consider the following statements: The Indian Parliament can invade the State List if 1. A subject in the State List assumes national importance 2. A proclamation of emergency has been issued by the President of India 3. The Supreme Court of India authorizes the Parliament to do so. Of these statements:
explanation: The Parliament can make law on the subjects mentioned in the State list when- • A proclamation of Emergency (Article 352) is in force. • Two or more states make a request to the Parliament to make a law on a subject. • Rajya Sabha passes a resolution that such subjects have acquired National Importance, to implement International treaties etc.

question: Concept of "Zero Hour" in Indian Parliamentary process means :

options.a: Intervening time between two different items on the agenda
options.b: Time between the Question Hour and the next item on the agenda
options.c: Time immediately after the Question Hour which is extended for more number of question-answer sessions at the discretion of the Speaker
options.d: None of the above statements is correct in this context

explanation: In Indian Parliamentary process 'Zero Hours' means the time between the question hour and the next item on the agenda. It is the time when Members of Parliament (MPs) can raise issues of Urgent Public Importance. For raising matters during the Zero Hour, MPs must give the notice before 10 am to the Speaker on the day of the sitting. The notice must state the subject they wish to raise in the House. However, Speaker of Lok Sabha, Rajya Sabha may allow or decline a Member to raise a matter of importance.

question: 'Power of Parliament to amend the Constitution and procedure thereof' – is given in ................. Article of Indian Constitution.
explanation: 'Power of Parliament to amend the Constitution and procedure is given in Article 368 of the Indian Constitution.

question: Residuary power is vested in
explanation: The residuary powers in the Indian Constitution are vested in the Parliament. The conduct of the legislative relations between the Union and the States is based on those three subjects. Which are named as Union list, State list and Concurrent list. These lists are placed in the Seventh Schedule. Parliament is empowered to make laws on all subjects in the Union list. The State legislatures generally have the right to make laws on the subjects of the State list. • The Concurrent list has importance from both regional and federal point of view. Both the Union and the States can make laws on the subjects of this list. If a conflict arises between the two laws, then the law of the Union shall prevail. • Apart from this, some such subjects remain which are not related to the three list, such subjects are kept in the residual list. According to Article 248 of the Constitution, Parliament has been given the right to make laws on such subjects.

question: The Parliament of India consist of
explanation: Legislature of the Union, which is called Parliament, consists of the President and two Houses, known as Council of States (Rajya Sabha) and House of the People (Lok Sabha). Rajya Sabha (Upper House) – 245 Members Lok Sabha (Lower House) – 543 Members

question: The purpose of Adjournment Motion in Parliament is :

options.a: Discussion on urgent matter of public importance
options.b: Oppositions attempt to collect information from Ministers
options.c: To demand reduction in the amount of a grant
options.d: To demand check on the violent behaviour of some members

explanation: The Primary objective of an adjournment motion is to draw the attention of the House to a recent matter of urgent public importance having serious consequences and with regard to which a motion or a resolution with proper notice will be too late.

question: One of the following statement is true about amending procedures in India-Identify.

options.a: Parliament has power to amend the Constitution of India
options.b: "Constitutional Convention" exists in India to amend the Constitution
options.c: The State can initiate Constitutional Amendments
options.d: Fundamental Rights cannot be amended Polity

explanation: Article 368 of Part XX of the Indian Constitution provides for two type of Amendment. • By Special majority of Parliament • By a Special majority of Parliament with ratification by half of total States. According to Article 368, the procedure for the Amendment of the Constitution is laid down in Article 368 as follows. • An Amendment of the Constitution can be initiated only by the introduction of a bill for the purpose in either House of Parliament (Lok Sabha & Rajya Sabha) and not in the State Legislatures. • The Bill can be introduced either by a minister or by private member and does not require prior permission of the President. • The Bill must be passed in each house by a special majority that is a majority of total members of the House present and voting. • Each House must pass Bill separately etc.

question: The power to levy an estate duty in respect of non-agricultural land belongs to
explanation: Parliament has power to levy an estate duty in respect of non-agricultural land.

question: The Parliament of Indian consists of :

options.a: The Lok Sabha, the Rajya Sabha and the office of the President of India only
options.b: The Lok Sabha and the Rajya Sabha only
options.c: The Lok Sabha, the Rajya Sabha and the office of the Prime Minister of India only
options.d: The Lok Sabha, the Rajya Sabha and the office of the President of India and the office of the Prime Minister of India.

explanation: The Parliament shall consist of the President and the two Houses known respectively as the Council of states (Rajya Sabha) and Houses of the people (Lok Sabha). • The Rajya Sabha is the upper Houses (second chamber or Houses of elders) and the Lok Sabha is the Lower House (first chamber or popular House). • The Maximum strength of the Rajya Sabha is fixed at 250. • The Maximum strength of the Lok Sabha is fixed at 552.

question: The President of India can be elected for -
explanation: The President of India under Article 56 (1) of the Indian Constitution holds office for a term of five years from the date on which he enters upon his office. There is no restriction of getting elected for restrictive periods. So he/she can be elected for any number of terms. 226 / 896

question: Who has the power to pardon in case of capital punishments?
explanation: • The President has power to pardon in case of Capital punishment. The Constitution of India under Article 72 empowers the President to grant Pardon, suspend, remit or commute sentences including the death sentence. A parallel power is also given to the Governor of the State under Article 161.

question: The executive power of the Union is vested in _____
explanation: As per Article 53(1), The executive power of the Union shall be vested in the President and shall be exercised by him either directly or through officers subordinate to him in accordance with the Constitution of India.

question: The President of India shall be elected by an electoral body consisting of :
explanation: As per Article 54 of the Constitution, The President is elected by an electoral college, which consists of the elected members of both houses of Parliament and the elected members of Legislative Assemblies of all the states and also of NCT (National Capital Territory) of Delhi and the Union Territory of Puducherry.

question: President of India submits his resignation to:
explanation: President of India submit his resignation to Vice President of India. • Article 56 of the Indian Constitution makes its clear that the term of the President is five years from the date on which he enters the office. His term may terminate earlier : By resignation, He may by writing and can resign addressing to the Vice President. Executive power of the union is vested in the President and is exercised by him directly or through officers subordinate to him in accordance with the Constitution. Polity

question: The Vice President of India may be removed from office

options.b: By the supreme court of India
options.c: By a resolution passed two third of the members of rajya sabha
options.d: By a resolution passed by a majority of members of rajya sabha and agreed to by lok sabha

explanation: According to Article 67 (b), A resolution by the Rajya Sabha passed by on effective majority and agreed by lok sabha by a simple majority the Vice President of India can be removed.

question: The President of India is bound to give the assent to the bill without reconsideration

options.b: Bill passed by the both house of Parliament

explanation: The President of India is bound to give the assent to the money bill without reconsideration. President can either accept or reject a money bill but can not return it for reconsideration. question: Who appoints the chairman of the Finance Commission?
explanation: The Finance Commission Chairman and its members appointed by the President. explanation: In Article 61 Procedure for Impeachment of the President. (cid:1) Article-61 (1) When a President is to be impeached for violation of the Constitution, the charge shall be preformed by either house of Parliament.

question: Who among the following had been the Vice- President of India for two full terms?
explanation: Sarvepalli Radhakrishnan served as the first vice-President of India (1952-1962) and the second President of India during 1962 to 1967. He was the only President of India who could not attend the Delhi Republic Day parade due to his ill health.

question: Which of the following Article empowers the President to grant pardon?
explanation: Article 72 of the Constitution empowers the President the power to grant pardons, reprieves, respites or remissions of punishment or to suspend, remit or commute the sentence of any person convicted of any offence.

question: In India, same person may be elected to the office of the President for
explanation: Article 52 of the Indian Constitution states that there shall be a President of India and President is the executive head of the state. President is elected by the collegiums consisting of elected members of the Lok Sabha Rajya Sabha and the legislative assemblies including the legislative assemblies of union territories. In India President can be elected for any number of team.

question: Who among the following is appointed as the Returning Officer in the President Elections?
explanation: The Election Commssion, in consultation, with the central Government, appoints the Secretary General of Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha, by rotation, as the Returning officer.

question: Which of the following Articles of the Constitution of India provides the procedure for impeachment of the President?
explanation: Article 61 of the Constitution of India provides the procedure for impeachment of the President. When a President is to be impeached for violation of the Constitution, the charge shall be preferred by either House of Parliament. No such charge shall be preferred unless.

question: Indicate the correct provision under Article-52 of the Constitution:

options.a: There shall be a Prime Minister.
options.b: There shall be a Council of Ministers.
options.c: There shall be a Governor for every state.
options.d: There shall be a President of India.

explanation: Article 52–The President of India is the head of the state and he symbolizes the entire nation.

question: According to the Indian Constitution who is the first citizen of the country?
explanation: According to the Indian Constitution, President is the first citizen of the country. Article 52 – There shall be a President of India. Article 54 – Election of the President. Article 57 – Eligibility for Re-election. Article 60 – Oath or Affirmation by the President. Article 61 – Procedure for impeachment of the President.

question: The executive power of the Indian Union and supreme command of defence forces of the Indian Union is vested in the:
explanation: The Executive power of the Indian Union and Supreme Command of Defence forces of the India Union is vested in the President as stated under Article 53 of India.

question: The dominant or preponderant position of one state over others in the international system is called :
explanation: The dominant or preponderant position of one state others in the international system is called hegemony. It is a leadership or dominance of one group over another eg.– student government leadership in a school. Caucus is a meeting of party leaders or party members to select candidates, elect convention delegates and establish the party's policy position on specific issues. Demagogue is a person or a political leader who wins support by exciting the emotions of the ordinary people rather than by having good or morally right ideas.

question: Assertions (A) : The President of India is the executive head of the state. Reason (R) : He is the Supreme Commander of the defence forces.

options.a: Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A).
options.b: Both (A) and (R) are true (R) is not correct explanation of (A).

explanation: The President of India is head of the Indian State. He is the first citizen of India and act as the symbol of unity integrity. All the executive actions of the Government of India are formally taken in his name. President is the Supreme Commander of the defence forces of India. He appoints the Chief of Army, the Navy and the Air Force. He can declares war or conclude peace, subject to the approval of the Parliament. Both the assertion and reason are correct but reason is not correct explanation of Assertion.

question: Assertions (A) : The President of India is the executive head of the state. Reason (R) : He is the Supreme Commander of the defence forces.

question: The procedure for the removal of Vice- President is given in which Article of Indian Constitution?
explanation: Article 63 of Indian Constitution states that "there shall be a Vice-President of India". Vice- President is the second highest Constitutional office in India after President. The procedure for the removal of Vice-President is given in Article 67 of Indian Constitution.

question: On whose advice does the President of India appoint a state governor?
explanation: The President of India appoints a state governor on the advice of the Union Council of Ministers, or in reality on the advice of Prime Minister (Articles 155). (cid:1) A person can be governor for two or more states and should be more than the age of 35 years.

question: Which one of the following statements are not correct?

options.a: The President can send message to the Parliament.
options.b: The President is not a part of the Parliament.
options.c: The President can summon each House of the Parliament
options.d: The President can dissolve the Lok Sabha 229 / 896

explanation: The Parliament is the highest legislative body in India. This is a bicameral system. The Indian Parliament consists of the President and the House - Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha. The President has the power to summon or adjourn either House of Parliament or dissolve the Lok Sabha. The President can send his message to both the Houses of Parliament.

question: In the Presidential system of Government the President is –

options.b: Head of the state and Head of the Government

explanation: In the Presidential system of Government, the President is Head of the state and Head of the Government. The President oversees the executive branch of government which includes the cabinet, or heads of various executive departments and various administrative bureaus and agencies.

question: The Vice-President of India is elected by
explanation: The Vice-President of India is elected by an electoral college consisting of members of both Houses of Parliament. Article - 66 of the Indian Constitution states the manner of Election of the Vice President.

question: When can President of India be impeached by the Parliament?

options.a: If he refuses to sign on a bill

explanation: According to Article 61 – The President may be removed before his tenure through impeachment for violating the Constitution of India by the parliament of India.

question: Who was the 3rd President of India?

question: On which hill the Rashtrapati Bhavan is situated in Delhi?
explanation: The Viceroy's house after the independence became the official Home of the President of India, located at the Western end of Rajpath in New Delhi, which is popularly known as Rashtrapati Bhavan. Rashtrapati Bhavan is situated at Raisina hills, on area of Lutyen's Delhi Prime Minister office and other important offices are also situated at Raisina Hills.

question: The Lokpal Act provides for investigation of corruption charges against Public functionaries. However it cannot investigate or initiate proceedings against .............. .
explanation: The Lokpal Act can not investigate or initiate proceedings against the President of India. Pinaki Chandra Ghose is the first Lokpal of India. The Lokpal and Lokayukta Act of 2013 established the Lokpal for the Union and Lokayukta for states. The Lokpal and Lokayukta are statutory bodies without any Constitutional status.

question: The President of India has recently signed Lokpal Act on January 1st 2014. The official name of Lokpal Act is ......... .
explanation: The Lokpal and Lokayuktas Act 2013, commonly known as the Lokpal Act, is an Anti Corruption Act of Indian Parliament in India which "seeks to provide for the establishment of the Institution of Lokpal to inquire into allegations of corruption against certain important public functionaries including the Prime Minister, Cabinet Ministers, Members of Parliament, Group A officials of the Central Government and for matters connecting them".

question: APJ Abdul Kakam served as President of India during
explanation: Avul Pakir Jainulabdeen Abdul Kalam was an Indian Scientist. He was the 11th President of India from 2002 to 2007. He is also known as "Missile man of India". He was awarded Bharat Ratna in 1997. His famous books are wings of fire, Ignited minds, India 2020 : A vision for the new millennium, The Luminous sparks etc. He was died on 27 July, 2015 aged of 83.

question: At National level a Ordinance refers to

options.a: Executive orders passed by the Parliament
options.b: Special Legislative powers of the President
options.c: Decision power by the Supreme Court
options.d: Ruling given by the speaker of the Lok Sabha

explanation: At National level a Ordinance refers to Special Legislative powers of the President. Article 123 of Indian Constitution grants the President certain law making powers to promulgate ordinances. Note : The Governor of a state can issue ordinances under Article 213 of the Indian Constitution. • Ordinance remains valid for 6 weeks from the date of passing of the ordinance. • If parliament does not pass the ordinance established within a time limit of six weeks after the commencement of the proceedings, the ordinance will automatically lapse.

question: The President of India may consult the Supreme Court under
explanation: Under Article 143 of Indian Constitution the President of India may obtain the opinion of the supreme court of India on any question or fact of law which may be of public importance and may need any advice from the Supreme Court. This process is also known as a Presidential reference. • Article 141 states that the law declared by the Supreme Court shall be binding on all courts within the territory of India. • Article 142 deals with Enforcement of decrees and orders of the Supreme Court. • Article 144 of the Constitution states that all authorities, civil & Judicial in the territory of India shall act in aid of the Supreme court.

question: The Electoral college which elects the President of India consists of :

options.a: Elected members of both the houses of Parliament only
options.b: Elected members of both the houses of Parliament and elected members of the state Legislative Assemblies

options.d: Members of the Parliament and State Legislatures

explanation: The President of India is Elected by the members of an electoral college consisting of • The elected members of both houses of parliament, • The elected members of the legislative assemblies of the states (Including National Capital Territory of Delhi and the Union Territory of Pondicherry vide the Constitution). Polity

question: President of India can be removed from office through :
explanation: Article 61- talks about procedure for impeachment. In India, the President can removed only for 'violation' of the Constitution and the Constitution does not define the meaning of the phrase 'violation' of the Constitution. The impeachment charges can be initiated by either house of parliament : Loksabha or Rajyasabha. • For this, the President has to give a notice singed by one-fourth of the members of that house 14 days ago. • Such resolution has been Passed by a Majority of not less than two thirds of the total Members of the house.

question: Vice-President of India is elected by :

options.c: By the members of both the houses of Parliament
options.d: By Parliament and Legislative Assemblies of the states

explanation: There is no direct election for vice President of India. The Election process is similar to that of President of India but the electoral college that elects President is different from the electoral college responsible for the election of Vice-President of India. For vice President election both the houses of parliament (Loksabha and Rajyasabha) take participation but not legislative assemblies of states. • The first Vice-President of India is Sarvepalli Radhakrishnan.

question: Which of the following statements about the President of India is correct?

options.a: The President of India shall not be a member of either House of parliament or of a House of the Legislature of any state
options.b: The President of India must be a member of Lok Sabha

He is called the first citizen of the country, head of all armed forces. He is the first citizen if the country and acts as the symbol of the integrity and solidarity of the nation.

question: Which of the following is NOT a part of the Electoral college to elect the President of India?

explanation: The Electoral college responsible for President's elections comprises elected members of- • Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha • Legislative assemblies of the states • Legislative assemblies of the union territories of Delhi and Puducherry.

question: The President's power to grant pardon is to be exercised

explanation: • Article 72 empowers the President to grant pardons, reprieves, respites or remissions of punishment or to suspend, remit or commute the sentence of any person convicted of any offence. • The President of India has to act on the advice of the cabinet. • Article 161, the Governor in India too has pardoning powers. • The President can grant pardon in all cases but the pardoning power of the Governor does not extend to death sentence cases.

question: The President enjoys pardoning powers under
explanation: According to Aricle 72, The President shall have the power to grant pardon, reprieve, respite or remission of punishment or to suspend, remit or commute the sentence of any person. • Article 73 provides that the executive power of the Union shall extend to the matters with respect to which parliament has power to make laws. • Article 74 states that there shall be a Council of Ministers to aid and advise President. • Article 75 of the Constitution States that the Prime Minister of India is appointed by the President.

question: "Doctrine of Pleasure" of President of India is associated with _____

question: Who was second President of India?
explanation: Dr. Sarvepalli Radhakrishnan (5 Sep. 1888 – 17 April 1975) was an Indian Philosopher and Stateman who was the first Vice President of India (1952–1962) and the Second President of India from 20th 1962 to 1967. One of India's most distinguished century scholars of comparative religion and philosophy, his academic appointments included Professor of Philosophy at the University of Mysore (1918–1921) the King George V Chair of Mental and Moral science at the University of Calcutta. • He was awarded Bharat Ratna in 1954.

question: Which is the biggest issue for the Government of India at Present?

question: The President of India can be removed from office by
explanation: Impeachment of the President– Under Article-61, The President may be removed before his tenure by impeachment for Violating the Constitution of India by the Parliament of India. The process may begin in either of the two houses of the Parliament.

question: The name of the candidate for the office of President of India may be proposed by
